data.name: Was ist Deutsch?
data.description: In this course we will examine some of the ideas and myths that became entangled with the emerging notion of a "German" identity in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. Some of the terms that we will discuss include 'Kultur,' 'Bildung', 'Freiheit' and 'Gesellschaft,' all of which have rich semantic histories. Conducted in German. Recommended prerequisite: one course in the GRMN 0500 series.

data.name: German Romanticism: Texts, Contexts, Legacies
data.description: An Introduction to the period of literary, artistic, and philosophical production known as German Romanticism. We will read some major texts of German Romanticism, but also discuss artistic, cultural, and political developments in the period. The goal is to understand why this particular moment in literary history has become such a pivotal one and why contemporary writers continue to draw on it. All texts read in translations. No German required.

data.name: Fragen und Antworten
data.description: Questions and answers haunt our everyday life. Rarely their co-appearance, when visiting us; rarely our co-habitation with them; is put––into question. And no answer responding to why the entire life, at times, seems to be a continuous Q&A-session, that wouldn't, immediately, provoke more questions. Questions and answers oscillate between violence and innocence, between mimesis and mockery, rhetorical, didactic and epistemological approaches. This seminar will pursue questions and answers in philosophical, literary and psychoanalytical texts (by Nietzsche, Frege, Wittgenstein; Hebel, Kafka; Freud), in fairy-tales ("Rumpelstilzchen") and poems (by Goethe and Rolf Dieter Brinkmann), in Plato's early dialogue Protagoras, in Bertolt Brecht's interrogation by the House Un-American Activities Committee (HUAC) on October 30 1947, in a scene from Robert de Niro's movie The Good Shepherd (2006), and in Jean Genet's last interview (BBC, summer 1985). –– Class Taught in German

data.name: Anti/formalisms Then and Now
data.description: This seminar examines the aesthetic and political stakes of form in film and visual arts by tracing formalist discourses from German idealism to the present. Beginning with Kant’s transcendental aesthetic and Hegel’s dialectic of form and matter, we will consider how Marx’s analysis of the value-form radicalizes formal thinking by linking aesthetics and critique. We will see how twentieth-century figures such as Warburg, Benjamin, Adorno, and Bataille reconfigured form as a historical, affective, and political process—or rather, project—before turning to contemporary theorists—including Bradley, Brinkema, Jackson, and Halberstam—who revisit and/or unsettle formalism through antiracist, feminist, queer, and materialist lenses. Films and art include works by American Artist, Hito Steyerl, Wu Tsang, Jacolby Satterwhite, Rosa von Praunheim, and more.

data.name: Bleibende Irritationen: Heinrich Heine und Deutschland
data.description: Heinrich Heine (1797-1856) has filled many roles in the history of German culture: a poet who wrote some of the most “Romantic” poems in the German language; an author who effortlessly switched between journalistic and literary writing; and a “wound” (Theodor W. Adorno) that cannot stop refusing to heal. We will conduct extensive readings from Heine’s poetic, essayistic, and narrative oeuvre which will result not only in a better understanding of the development of post-classical German literature, but also in a deeper knowledge of German culture as a whole.

data.name: On Being With the Dead in Art, Literature, and Philosophy
data.description: This seminar will explore the idea that to be alive in an emphatic sense is to live not only with the living but also with the dead. This way of being challenges us continually to reinterpret our relationship with the dead and to engage with them in a spectral dialogue. The dead demand to be treated responsibly in this exchange, even though—or precisely because—they are no longer among the living. But our ways of being with the dead always also pertain to ourselves and our future. After contemplating Heidegger’s reflections on being with the dead in Being and Time, we will conduct a series of case studies drawn from art and critical thought, including Derrida’s “Life-Death” seminar, Barthes’ Mourning Diary, the prose and poetry of Kafka, Rilke, and Celan, Adorno’s reflections on art “after Auschwitz,” and other works.

data.name: Dream-Work; or, the Poetics of Thought
data.description: In his seminar on the Formations of the Unconscious, Jacques Lacan remarks: “If there's anything that the Freudian experience tells us, it is that we are determined by laws in the very depths of ourselves, as we rightly or wrongly say, adopting an image —let's simply say, at the level of what lies within us beyond our conceptual grasp of ourselves, beyond the idea that we have of ourselves and draw upon.” Those ‘laws’ align, before all else, with the functions of condensation and displacement that Freud elaborates in the Interpretation of Dreams, and that draw what Freud calls the ‘Dream-Work’ (Traumarbeit) into closest proximity with the work of poetry. Departing from Freud’s Interpretation of Dreams, we will examine the poetics of thought through both psychoanalysis and literary analysis, with readings by writers such as Paul Celan, Sandor Ferenczi, Sigmund

data.name: Elementary Hebrew
data.description: This is the second half of a year-long course, an introduction to the skills of reading, writing, and conversing in contemporary Israeli Hebrew. Students also read Hebrew texts adapted for their level of Hebrew based on biblical, rabbinic, and modern Hebrew literature, which introduce them to the approaches of Hebrew writers in various periods and to a variety of cultural issues. Prerequisite: HEBR 0100. Students must have taken HEBR 0100 for credit to receive credit for this course. Exceptions must be approved by both the academic department and the Committee on Academic Standing. Enrollment limited to 20.

data.name: Intermediate Hebrew
data.description: Develops the skills of reading, writing, and conversing in contemporary Israeli Hebrew at the intermediate level and of reading Hebrew texts of the biblical, rabbinic, and modern periods (biblical stories, rabbinic legends, modern Hebrew poems, stories, essays, newspaper articles). Discussions and compositions focus on the psychological, cultural, political, and social issues reflected in the Hebrew sources that we study. Prerequisite: HEBR 0300 or equivalent. Enrollment limited to 20. If unable to enroll because of closed registration please contact the professor and a wait list will be created.

data.name: The Repeating Island: Visual Cultures of the Early Modern Caribbean
data.description: Considered in many ways the birthplace of the modern world, the Caribbean has long been central to the study of capitalism, slavery, world economic systems, race, class, gender, and environmental transformation. This course explores the visual and material cultures of the Greater Caribbean through close study of objects, texts, and images. Topics include Indigenous art, colonial architecture and cartography, the plantation landscape, slavery and its representations, Afro-Caribbean religions, urban cultures, natural history, and the visual legacies of the Haitian Revolution. Emphasizing close looking and archival engagement, the course draws on local collections—including the Haffenreffer Museum of Anthropology, the John Carter Brown Library, and the Museum of Fine Arts, Boston—while asking how the Caribbean’s visual culture, often difficult to see or categorize, can be recovered within art history.

data.name: Introduction to American Art: The Twentieth Century
data.description: This course examines fine art in the US from roughly 1900 through the 1970s, with special attention to modernism and the rise of modern art. Through slide presentations and group discussion, most major figures of the period—Georgia O'Keeffe, Edward Hopper, Alexander Calder, Jackson Pollock, and Andy Warhol among them—will be placed in historical and cultural context. Key events, institutions, and critics of these decades will also be considered. We will look at the role of cultural episodes like the Harlem Renaissance, and the increasing prevalence of women in the art world, as markers of increasing diversity and changing values. Readings by leading scholars will allow a wide-ranging introduction to art historical methods of interpretation. Discussion in sections will help place this art within the context of social history, the invention of national identity, and parallel developments in popular visual culture.

data.name: Revolutions, Illusions, Impressions: A History of Nineteenth-Century Art
data.description: Over the long nineteenth century (1789-1900) revolutions replaced kings with citizens. Capitalist and colonial expansion mobilized armies, goods, and slaves across continents. New class and gender dynamics changed patterns of sociability. Technological innovations mass produced images and goods. In this course, we will discern such social and historical factors in Europe and across the globe alongside artistic ones to interrogate what makes art in this period irrepressibly modern. We will study the turbulence that has defined the century, including the art historical swings in style from Classicism to Romanticism to Impressionism, with care. Course includes visits and assignments in museums.

data.name: Contemporary Art
data.description: This lecture course will survey the major movements and artistic strategies of contemporary art since 1989. Topics include installation art, new documentary, performance/re-performance, new genre public art, relational aesthetics, arte útil, critical geography, post-production, para-fiction, research-based art, and post-colonial, post-critical, and post-internet practices. As suggested by the prevalence of “posts,” our discussion will center on the persistence of history in the art of the present.

data.name: Introduction to Architectural Design Studio
data.description: The class introduces students to basic tools and strategies in architectural design. A warmup exercise and several design assignments guide students to explore questions about form, function, structure, and light. The semester is devoted to the design of a small house. Students are trained in rigorous, conceptual thinking and graphically and verbally clear pronunciation of consequential design logic. By the end of the semester, the proposed building design is presented in concept diagrams, plans, sections, elevations, and a model. Course is intended for first and second year students. Preference is given to actual and future architecture concentrators. To apply for this class, students are required to submit an override request and a note via CAB to specify their concentration, semester level, and previous applications for this class. Enrollment limited to 15.

Enrollment limited to 15.

data.name: Architectural Projection
data.description: This course introduces the beginning student to the origins, media, geometries and role(s) of projection drawing in the design and construction process. The student will learn systems of projection drawing from direct experience, and be challenged to work both from life and to life. Subjects such as transparency, figure/ground, sciagraphy, oblique projection, surface development, volumetric intersections, spatial manipulation and analytic operations will build on the basics of orthographic and conic projection. The course involves line and tone drawing, hand drafting, computer drawing(Autocad) and computer modeling(Rhino).

data.name: Intermediate Architectural Design Studio
data.description: Building on the skills taught in the Introductory Studio, this studio emphasizes design as a mode of critical cultural inquiry to explore the cultural, environmental, and political forces which shape relationships between cities and the buildings, landscapes that form the public realm. Questions of the meaning of materials, functions, and urban imagery will be addressed with particular emphasis on acquiring greater fluency with the graphic tools and iterative processes through which designers conceptualize, synthesize, and represent the complex conditions of site, program, tectonics, and materiality to create urban landscapes. The city of Providence usually serves as a case study for observation and testing ground for your ideas, in addition to providing a site for your imaginary project. Students will come away with enhanced proficiency in representational techniques and a greater awareness of the social conditions of architectural production.

data.name: Contemporary Architecture
data.description: Stylistic, technological, and theoretical developments in architecture from the 1960s to the present. Analyzes movements such as "Brutalism," "Postmodernism," and "Deconstruction" and works by architects such as Frank Gehry, I. M. Pei, and Zaha Hadid. Emphasizes the complex conditions of architectural production in different parts of the world. Complements HIAA 0850, but may be taken independently. A

data.name: Museums and the Management of Mesoamerican Art
data.description: Museums act as both a window into the world and a reflection of the cultures that create them. The way in which museums create, organize, and present their collections communicates a message that visitors absorb, whether consciously or subconsciously. In this seminar, students explore how museums both at home and abroad have engaged with objects from the ancient Americas, with particular emphasis on Mesoamerica. Topics of discussion include museum collection practices, repatriation, forgery and authenticity, multicultural representation, and curation and exhibition practices. At the end of the semester, students will submit a virtual exhibit they have designed themselves, along with an essay that justifies their object and design choices.

data.name: To Teach, Delight, and Stir the Soul: Theatricality and the Spectator in Early Modern Rome (1600-175
data.description: Across the streets, churches, and palaces of Rome, artists, patrons and popes harnessed the persuasive power of art to “to teach, delight, and stir the soul” of spectators. This seminar explores the professed theatricality of art produced in seventeenth-century Rome and the strategies used to engage and absorb the viewer. Examples of painting, sculpture, print, and architecture will be considered in the context of Counter-Reformation reforms and emerging artistic theories such as Bernini’s bel composto and Bellori’s Idea. Modern theoretical discussions about theatricality and spectatorship in visual art will also be considered. Students will also be asked to consider the lasting impact of Baroque art and theatricality today. A

data.name: Art Criticism Workshop
data.description: This seminar broaches the how and why of art criticism: how it is written and why it is undertaken at all. We will investigate the ways in which well written art criticism brings readers into deep relation with visual artwork, and into a dialogue with the histories, values, and concerns that undergird its making. We will examine how criticism provides a window through which we can examine ourselves and our cultural ideals, locating ourselves along the axes of our collective fears, desires, and ambitions. Learning goals consist of absorbing how to employ the methods and tools needed to produce writing that renders a judgment regarding the historical, aesthetic, intellectual, emotional, cultural, or even financial value of a work of art.

data.name: Codicology Compared
data.description: This seminar will critically explore select issues in the study of codicology across Christian and Islamic traditions, particularly in the Mediterranean Basin and adjacent areas of contact. Not a chronological survey, the seminar will instead investigate topics of current art historical and historiographic significance, including: the relationships between manuscript painting and textual traditions and sources; cultures of reading; the impact of religious practice on visual programs; modes of book production and reception; histories of book collecting and provenance; the rise of the professional illuminator in relationship to changes in the locales; along with the methods and means of book production.

data.name: Spanish Art in the Age of Empire
data.description: This course examines Spanish artistic culture in the 16th and 17th centuries, when Spain ruled the Low Countries, parts of Italy, the Americas, and the Philippines. We will study foreign-born painters active in Spain (e.g., Sofonisba Anguissola and El Greco),Spanish artists who worked abroad (such as José Ribera), the court painter Diego Velázquez—raised in the global trading capital of Seville—and others. Taken together, their art encompassed intensely religious imagery and mythological scenes, humble still lifes and magnificent royal portraits—many on view at a major exhibition at the Boston Museum of Fine Arts in spring 2026.

data.name: Buildings, Space, Power: Postcolonial and Decolonial Histories of the Built Environment
data.description: This seminar examines the state of the field in historical studies of modern colonialism and imperialism and built environments. By reading and discussing recent publications in architectural history, urban studies, anthropology, history, literary studies, and colonial/ postcolonial studies, the course identifies new approaches in the field, including a focus on intersections between colonialism and ecological disaster, development and the redistribution of land during and after colonialism, distinctive forms of labor that emerged in colonial built environment contexts, consumption practices and their role in nurturing new markets and new social classes, and strategies for navigating the contemporary material legacies of colonialism such as monuments and toponymy. Our study revolves around two principal questions: How was the built environment leveraged to support and sometimes challenge colonial and imperial ideologies? In turn how have postcolonial societies worked to transform colonial built discourses? A

data.name: The Art and Architecture of Brown University
data.description: This seminar explores Art and Architecture on Brown’s campus as witnesses to our university’s history. We will update the existing Brown Facades App, incorporating new buildings since the app’s first version of 2013, and our public art from Marcus Aurelius to the Slavery Memorial. Course texts will reflect Brown’s involvement in U.S. political history – the student boycott of the List Art Building construction in 1970, alumni such as John Hay, US Secretary of State or Samuel Gridley Howe, who served in the Greek War of Independence (commemorated at Brown’s flagpole). In the process of this seminar, students will be introduced to a range of research methods, to critical thinking and concise writing, and to many facets of Brown’s and US history. They will learn to observe, analyze and describe a building or a work of art. A

data.name: Criticality and Modern Art
data.description: This seminar will interrogate a central way of understanding the politics of twentieth-century art: criticality. The notion that art’s relationship to society is one of negation, diagnosis, and destabilization pervades modern art history. This seminar will trace the development of criticality as an artistic practice and art-historical methodology. Alongside texts on critique in philosophy and theory, we will examine artists for whom criticality is paramount: Dada’s ballistic mission, repetitions of the neo-avant-garde, anti-art, institutional critique, critical postmodernism, art as undercommons. We will end with recent claims of critique’s collapse and assess the viability of criticality in art of today.

data.name: Buildings, Space, Power: Postcolonial and Decolonial Histories of the Built Environment
data.description: This seminar examines the state of the field in historical studies of modern colonialism and imperialism and built environments. By reading and discussing recent publications in architectural history, urban studies, anthropology, history, literary studies, and colonial and postcolonial studies, the course identifies new approaches in the field, including a focus on intersections between colonialism and ecological disaster, development and the redistribution of land during and after colonialism, distinctive forms of labor that emerged in colonial built environment contexts, consumption practices and their role in nurturing new markets and new social classes, and strategies for navigating the contemporary material legacies of colonialism such as monuments and toponymy. Our study revolves around two principal questions: How was the built environment leveraged to support and sometimes challenge colonial and imperial ideologies? In turn, how have postcolonial societies worked to transform colonial built discourses?

data.name: Asian Reprographics A Long History of Impression
data.description: This seminar examines the early history of reprography in East Asia. Defining reprography broadly to encompass all pre-photographic technologies of graphic impression, it explores the transfers that occurred within and between piece-mold bronze casting, ceramic molding, sealing, rubbing, and woodblock printing as they developed in succession and tandem over the past four millennia. In particular, the seminar considers the extent to which technics of transfer facilitated the movement of images across medium and time.

data.name: Artists and the East India Company
data.description: This collaborative seminar examines artists’ innovations amidst the commercial ambitions of the East India Company at major ports and centers of trade across Asia. Within the period of the Company’s relentless pursuit of profits, artists trained in India, China, and Britain forged relationships across cultures and experimented with materials and techniques to produce striking artworks. This course interrogates the relationship between these artists and a corporation. It asks whether “style” links such disparate arts through subjects of colonial interest—including portraits and ethnographic types, landscapes and architectural drawings, plants and animals—or if the description holds only through the terminology of “Company” art that developed as the British empire waned. Students will engage with these arts in Brown’s collections, and in the exhibition, Painters, Ports & Profits: Artists and the East India Company, 1760-1830, at the Yale Center for British Art.

data.name: Criticality and Modern Art
data.description: This seminar will interrogate a central way of understanding the politics of twentieth-century art: criticality. The notion that art’s relationship to society is one of negation, diagnosis, and destabilization pervades modern art and its historiography. This seminar will trace the development of criticality as both an artistic practice and art-historical methodology. Alongside foundational texts on critique in philosophy and cultural theory, we will examine artists for whom criticality is paramount: Dada’s ballistic mission, repetitions of the neo-avant-garde, antiart, institutional critique, critical postmodernism, art as undercommons. We will end with recent claims of critique’s collapse and assess the viability of criticality in art of today.

data.name: Basic Spanish
data.description: A continuation of HISP 0100. This course continues to focus on acquisition of communicative skills (speaking, listening comprehension, reading and writing) as well as cultural awareness. With successful completion of the course students will be able to understand simple texts, carry on short spontaneous conversations involving everyday topics (such as modern day life and its pressures, health, art and culture, nature and the environment, relationships) and write simple texts with good command of grammar and sentence structure. Prerequisites: HISP 0100, Brown placement score: 241 - 340. Note that all students who enter the Spanish program should take the Brown placement test. Students should check Placement and Course Description in the Undergraduate Program section of the Hispanic Studies Website. Enrollment limited to 15; 12 spaces are available for students during

data.name: Intermediate Spanish I
data.description: This course involves about 14-15 hours of work/week. Prerequisites: HISP 0200, 0110. Brown placement score: 341 - 410. Note that all students who enter the Spanish program should take the Brown placement test. It carries on the work initiated in HISP0110-100-200 to develop and strengthen students' linguistic, communicative, academic, and multicultural competencies. It continues to focus on the integration of grammar, vocabulary, and discourse work to advance competence and proficiency in Spanish and to support further development of communication in all the modalities. This course is framed by an inclusive perspective on learning and embraces diverse identities and communities in the Hispanic World. It fosters a community of learning among students and offers a variety of texts, themes, and topics related to students’ academic and life experiences that also help them develop professional skills. Enrollment is limited to 12.

data.name: Intermediate Spanish II
data.description:
A continuation of HISP 0300. This course continues to develop and strengthen students’ linguistic, communicative, academic, and multicultural competencies. It focuses on content and language integration and creates opportunities to use the language in interdisciplinary scenarios related to diverse academic experiences. Enrollment is limited to 15. Pre-enrolled students must attend the first four days of class to maintain their pre-registered status and notify the instructor in advance if they must miss any day before the 4th class when the composition of the course section is finalized.

Prerequisites: HISP 0300, Brown placement score: 411 - 490. Note that all students who enter the Spanish program should take the Brown placement test. Students should check Placement and Course Description in the Undergraduate Program section of the Hispanic Studies Website.

data.name: Intermediate Spanish II
data.description:
A continuation of HISP 0300. This course continues to develop and strengthen students’ linguistic, communicative, academic, and multicultural competencies. It focuses on content and language integration and creates opportunities to use the language in interdisciplinary scenarios related to diverse academic experiences. Enrollment is limited to 15. Pre-enrolled students must attend the first four days of class to maintain their pre-registered status and notify the instructor in advance if they must miss any day before the 4th class when the composition of the course section is finalized.

Prerequisites: HISP 0300, Brown placement score: 411 - 490. Note that all students who enter the Spanish program should take the Brown placement test. Students should check Placement and Course Description in the Undergraduate Program section of the Hispanic Studies Website.

data.name: Advanced Spanish I
data.description: Offers comprehensive work in listening, speaking, reading, and writing, with targeted grammar review. Students work with a variety of readings (literature, newspaper articles, etc.) and with art forms such as music and film, in order to develop oral and written expression and to explore issues relevant to the Hispanic world. Students explore topics of their own interest through student-led activities and presentations. Prerequisites: HISP 0400, 0490A, Brown placement score: 491 - 570. Note that all students who enter the Spanish program should take the Brown placement test. Please check Hispanic Studies website (Undergraduate Programs) for course descriptions and placement information. Enrollment limited to 15; 12 spaces are available for students during pre-registration. 3 spaces will be available at the start of the semester for incoming or re-admitted students who should attend the first class.

data.name: Advanced Spanish II
data.description: Advanced-level work in speaking, listening, reading, and writing, with focused review of challenging Spanish grammar. Course materials include films, music, art works, and a variety of written texts chosen to promote class discussion and in-depth written analysis. There will be individual and group activities, including in-class presentations and creative writing projects. Prerequisites: HISP 0500, Brown placement score: 571 - 650. Note that all students who enter the Spanish program should take the Brown placement test. Enrollment limit 12. Pre-enrolled students must attend the first four classes to maintain their status and notify the instructor in advance if they must miss any day before the 4th class when the course section is finalized.

data.name: Advanced Spanish Through Literature & Film
data.description: HISP 0650 aims to study literature and film from Latin America and Spain through critical reading and analytical writing. This section is thematic: the majority of its contents revolve around the representation of the self. We will read first person literature, autobiographies, diaries, and myriad other genres, focusing on how they inform our notions of memory, authorship and identity. The course will serve as an introduction to the literatures and cultures of the Spanish-speaking world, covering a range of periods and genres (the story, the novel, poetry, film, and non-fiction). Beyond offering a historical and contextualized panorama of cultural production from the Spanish-speaking world, HISP 0650 will also help you develop new strategies for reading, writing, and thinking about literature and film, preparing you for more advanced literature and culture courses at the 1000 level.

data.name: Intensive Survey of Spanish Literature
data.description: HISP740, Intensive Survey of Spanish Literature, provides students with an overview of Spain’s major authors and literary movements from the Middle Ages to the present day. This course offers a varied introduction to Spanish literature in its historical and cultural contexts through the reading and analysis of poetry, essay, theater, and narrative fiction. There are two principal objectives for this class: a) to expose students to Spain’s cultural production and familiarize them with both canonical as well as non-canonical texts; b) to teach students to critically engage with and analyze literature in its historical and culture context. Reading chronologically, students will be able to appreciate the development of the Spanish literary tradition as they ask critical questions about identity, race, the nation, and cultural formation. Conducted in Spanish.

data.name: The Latin American Diaspora in the US
data.description: This engaged course is designed to bridge academic learning about Latin American diasporic history and culture and volunteer work in agencies serving Latinx communities in Providence. Readings, films, and guest presentations examine the ways in which the Latinx community has imagined itself and has been imagined by others. Spanish language learning occurs in the classroom and the community, where students have the opportunity to enrich and test course content. Conducted in Spanish, Spanglish welcome. Prerequisite: HISP 0600 or placement: SAT II scores of over 750, 5 in AP Literature or 651 and over in the Brown Placement Exam.

data.name: Health, Illness and Medicine in Spanish and Spanish American Literature and Film
data.description: In this class we will read/see, discuss and write about texts and films that deal with health, illness, death and medicine in Spanish and Spanish American contexts. Our approach will be informed by principals of Narrative Medicine that demonstrate how attending to, representing and affiliating oneself with other human beings by studying literature and the arts can transform relationships between patients and healthcare professionals. We will be honing our reading and analytical skills as we confront the subjective dimensions of illness and medicine from humanistic and cross-cultural perspectives. This course is conducted in Spanish.

data.name: Spanish Art in the Age of Empire (HIAA 1070)
data.description: Interested students must register for HIAA 1070.

data.name: A Nation in its Labyrinth: An Introduction to Colombia
data.description: Tobacco, Rubber, Bananas, Coffee, Coca, Orchids… The image of Colombia has traveled the world under the guise of tropical products linked to speed, pleasure, violence and exoticism. But what lies behind the deceiving, magical spell of these traveling products? This course will travel back from the product to the place where these plants were tended to and flourished. The works of renowned artists such as García Márquez and lesser-known masters like Marvel Moreno or Débora Arango will allow us to read against the grain the history of a country crisscrossed by the diversity of its people and the beauty of its environments. Even though the histories of violence and social conflict will traverse this course, so will the warm sounds of cumbia, the voices of its poets and the green joy of its landscapes.

data.name: Medicine and Disability in Shakespeare and Early Modern Drama (COLT 1411F)

data.name: Medieval Masterpieces
data.description: Examines three medieval Spanish masterpieces: Cantar de Mio Cid, Libro de buen amor, and Celestina. Other works are read to explore lines of continuity and discontinuity in these three works and their respective genres.

data.name: Emergence of the Vernacular: Prestige and Critique (Spanish & Italian)
data.description: Using pertinent sources drawn primarily from Latin, Spanish, Italian, and their early variants, this class will study the emergence and development of the vernacular from its beginnings through the Early Modern period. We will consider theorizations of vernacularity alongside some of its most pressing debates as they circulated throughout Spain, Italy, and the Americas, in particular. To this end, we will examine historical sources that exemplify the wide reach of the sociopolitical, linguistic, and identitary anxieties and violences surrounding linguistic development, while concentrating our attentions on literary sources (prose, drama, poetry) and didactic manuals and epistles that directly comment upon the status of language(s), vilifying, defending, redefining, or reaffirming them. Students should be proficient in either Spanish or Italian, and have advanced reading abilities in the other.

data.name: Exploring and Extracting in Colonial Latin American Literatures
data.description: This graduate seminar provides a panorama of Latin American literatures during the period covered by the Colonial preliminary exam list. Historiographically, this period has been termed Europe’s age of exploration. From the Americas, however, it could be better characterized as the age of extraction. We will probe the writings of key authors and texts from Colonial Latin America on the tension between exploring (associated with the deeply human capacity for curiosity and wonder) and extracting (the result of the also very human impulse to possess and accumulate). In our readings, we will see how at times the two are indistinguishable (a slippage that is evident in the Portuguese “exploraçao” meaning “exploitation”); at others, we will glimpse at the authors’ desire to replenish landscapes, restitute rights and reimagine worldviews beyond the regimes brought forth by the adjective “Colonial”. Conducted in Spanish.

data.name: The Ocean in Global History
data.description: This course examines how the ocean and its denizens have influenced and been shaped by diverse social, material, political, and cultural factors across different spaces and chronologies. We will consider how historical actors across global history have approached the ocean and its creatures as sources of sustenance and power, cosmology and knowledge, conveyance and death. We will weigh, too, how adopting an oceanic perspective can open new ways of understanding the past, present, and future of our planet and its inhabitants. Topics considered include contemporary and historic Indigenous seafaring traditions and maritime subjectivities across Moana/Pacific and other oceanic basins; Atlantic seaborne empires, piracy, human trafficking in the age of sail; the establishment of oceanography as a scholarly discipline; the evolution of maritime transportation infrastructure under global capitalism; the political ecology of a warming ocean in the era of climate crisis.

data.name: Curators, Hoarders, and Looters: The Long and Curious History of Collecting
data.description: What does it mean to collect? What drives human cultures to amass, hoard, steal, select, separate and display objects? This undergraduate lecture course – at the intersection of anthropology, geography, history, and museum studies – examines forms and practices of collecting from antiquity to the present. We will explore museums, archives, libraries, and other less formal institutions from around the globe, probing in the process the disciplinary boundaries by which scholars have sought to understand the implications of collections in different times and distant territories. We will unpack libraries, visit early modern cabinets of curiosities, and gain insights into Indigenous collections: from Polynesia to Mashantucket. And we will address the fraught yet fascinating relationship between the collection, the catalogue, and the archive – and the gathering, registering, and organizing that constitute them.

data.name: Southeast Asian History
data.description: From Melaka to Manila, and from sultanates to megacities, this new course introduces students to the plural past of Southeast Asia. In doing so, it analyzes how the region came to be (as a field of study in the mid-twentieth century), while surveying, too, the ways in which histories of polity, port, empire, and revolution have shaped not only the world in Southeast Asia—but also Southeast Asia in the world.

data.name: Modern Latin America
data.description: This course is an introduction to the history of modern Latin America. Through lectures, discussions, shared readings, we will explore major themes in the past two hundred years of Latin American history, from the early nineteenth-century independence movements to the recent “Left Turn” in Latin American politics. Some of the topics we will examine include the racial politics of state-formation; the fraught history of U.S.-Latin American relations; the cultural politics of nationalism; how modernity was defined in relation to gender and sexuality; and the emergence of authoritarian regimes and revolutionary mobilizations, and the role of religion in shaping these processes.

data.name: The American Civil War
data.description: This course, covering the period roughly from 1820-1865 and focusing mostly on 1860-1865, examines the origins of the Civil War, the war itself, and preliminary efforts at reconstruction during wartime. The class meetings and readings will focus on the transformation wrought by the war, investigating in particular certain issues relevant to current affairs: 1) enslavement, emancipation, and struggles for equality; 2) sectionalism, secession, and insurrection; 3) the role of law in the prosecution and resolution of war; and 4) the role of the Civil War in American popular culture and politics. There are no prerequisites--the course is accessible to students at all levels--but some knowledge of U.S. history might be useful.

data.name: Introduction to Latinx History
data.description: This course provides a comprehensive historical examination of Latinx people in the United States. Students will explore major questions, theory, and research methods pertinent to the historical narratives of Mexican Americans, Puerto Ricans, Dominican Americans, Cuban Americans, and Central Americans. Major topics of study include immigration, ethnic identity formation, social and political activism, labor, and articulations of race, gender, and culture in urban and rural settings. This course ultimately demonstrates how the Latinx experience is vital to our understanding of the social, economic, and cultural history of the United States.

data.name: History of Artificial Intelligence
data.description: The course will trace the origins and trajectory of ideas about artificial intelligence, starting with the “active intellect” of Aristotle, early analog computers and automata, Ada Lovelace’s “calculus of the nervous system,” through the “general intellect” and “machine capital” of Karl Marx, Karel Čapek’s “Universal Robots,” the “Turing test,” “cyberpositivity” and Donna Haraway’s “Cyborg Manifesto,” and concepts like “swarm intelligence,” “hive mind,” “singularity,” and the “master algorithm.” Sources will encompass a range of disciplinary approaches (philosophy, sociology, computer science, etc.), formats (text, film, graphic novel), and genres including Japanese anime and Afrofuturism.

data.name: Welfare States and a History of Modern Life
data.description: History of the American welfare state, from its origins in nineteenth-century industrial capitalism to contemporary debates about health care, in comparative perspective. Why did welfare states appear and what form did the U.S. version take? Considerations of social inequality, labor relations, race, gender, family policy, the social wage, and the relationship between markets and the state are all considered. Some comparison with European models.

data.name: Unnatural Disasters: Rethinking Histories of Catastrophe in the Americas
data.description: Since Hurricane Katrina, the history of natural disasters and the new interdisciplinary field of critical disaster studies have surfaced. Both fields of agree that natural disasters are windows onto the societies they strike and are processes emerging from historical human and nonhuman causes. Natural disasters, they agree, are unnatural. Studying a range of environmental processes from melting glaciers in the Andes to radioactive pollution west of Rockies, this course adopts a hemispheric scope to connect geographical, temporal, and thematic fields of history. We will think together about the agency of American environments and the hazards that haunt them. We draw on diverse methods to examine the roles that race, power, indigeneity, disability, gender, and science play in the history of (un)natural disasters. These methods will help us analyze primary sources while also encouraging curiosity for overlooked American communities and environments.

data.name: Indigenous Education
data.description: This course examines contemporary Indigenous issues through the lens of education. Why were colonial schools established? What was the legacy of colonial schooling? How has reconciliation been sought? How have Indigenous communities responded? Students will examine current issues and how they relate to historical processes such as Indigenous epistemologies; the origins, development, decline, and legacy of the federal residential, boarding, and day school system; the meaning of apologies; and the importance of language revitalization. Additionally, students will study how Indigenous activists, storytellers, and artists have used colonial mediums to combat colonialism and resist epistemicide: the eradication of Indigenous knowledge. Students will use discussion periods to analyze and reflect on their own lives and how their gender, ethnicity, and privilege relates to, contributes to, or challenges, Indigenous issues. Topics/themes include colonization, treaties, justice, truth and reconciliation, Indigenous leadership, and Indigenous resistance.

data.name: Knowledge and Power: Material Culture History of Tibetan Buddhism during the Qing Dynasty 17-19th c
data.description: The mid-seventeenth century ushered in a series of momentous political changes in China and Tibet. The Dalai Lama-led Ganden Phodrang state emerged in Lhasa with the ambitious ruler and his coterie displaying their power through new art and infrastructure. Further east, the Manchus, despite being a minority in Confucian China, rose to become the Qing emperors in Beijing. As they expanded the empire’s territories to include the Inner Asian regions of Mongolia and Tibet, they projected a complex model of Buddhist kingship to gain legitimacy among the new subjects. How are these historical developments reflected in the material and print culture of this period (17th-19th centuries)? Each week, we will cover one theme of Buddhist material culture. Topics include book publishing and written technology; monastic architecture and replication of infrastructure; creation of statues, paintings and ritual objects among others.

data.name: Postwar Japan
data.description: This course is for students interested in exploring Japan’s remarkable cultural, political and social transformations from the closing days of the Second World War, through its emergence as an apparent exemplar of democratization’s potential and capitalism’s benefits, and on to the contemporary era. Lectures, readings and films will explore the legacies of the war and the Occupation, the so-called “economic miracle” (and its effects on the environment), the protest movements of the 1960s and beyond, and Japan’s complicated relationships with its neighbors, with the U.S., and with its own recent history. Open to all students.

data.name: The Viking Age
data.description: For two centuries, Viking marauders struck terror into hearts of European Christians. Feared as raiders, Norsemen were also traders and explorers who maintained a network of connections stretching from North America to Baghdad and who developed a complex civilization that was deeply concerned with power and its abuses, the role of law in society, and the corrosive power of violence. This class examines the tensions and transformations within Norse society between AD 750 and 1100 and how people living in the Viking world sought to devise solutions to the challenges that confronted them as their world expanded and changed. P

data.name: The Global British Empire, 1600-The Present
data.description: This course charts the long history and continuing legacies of the British Empire, an entity that has transformed every single continent over the last four centuries and is widely associated with the makings of the modern world. We examine how and why a powerful and expansive British Empire emerged and sustained itself. Equally, we zoom in on the regular contestation and even outright rebellion that this transcontinental polity inspired. This course is an opportunity to think connectively and comparatively about historical experiences in America, India, the Caribbean, and Africa among multiple other British imperial spaces. We will track the making and unmaking of the British Empire by studying the evolution of global trade, labor regimes including slavery, the consumption of commodities such as sugar, as well as new ideas about governance, race, and identity.

data.name: Paris: Sacred and Profane, Imagined and Real
data.description: Paris has been called the capital of modernity, the capital of the nineteenth century, and the capital of the black Atlantic. This course explores how Paris grew from a small settlement into a vast city with an enormous global impact. Covering the settlement of the Celtic Parisii in the mid-third century BCE through the present, the course investigates the dynamic relationship between urban space, public activism, racism, and colonialism. It also considers who has been excluded from the city’s complex mythology and how these myths impacted experiences of the “other” (including people of color, low-income people, Jewish people, and women).

data.name: History of the Andes
data.description: Before the Spanish invaded in the 1530s, western South America was the scene of the largest state the New World had ever known, Tawantinsuyu, the Inca empire. During almost 300 years of colonial rule, the Andean provinces were shared by the "Republic of Spaniards" and the "Republic of Indians" - two separate societies, one dominating and exploiting the other. Today the region remains in many ways colonial, as Quechua- and Aymara-speaking villagers face a Spanish-speaking state, as well as an ever-more-integrated world market, the pressures of neoliberal reform from international banks, and the melting of the Andean glaciers.

data.name: The American Revolution
data.description: This course will explore the period of the American Revolution from the 1760s through the turn of the nineteenth century. Taking a broad view of the conflict and its consequences, we will situate the American colonies in their North American and Atlantic context, examine the material and ideological concerns that prompted the Revolutionary War, and trace the consequences of the conflict for the nation that followed. Students will be invited to look beyond the Founders to the experiences of women, slaves, Native Americans, common soldiers, and Loyalists.

data.name: Cities and Inequality Since 1920: The United States
data.description: This lecture course takes up the relationship between cities and social and economic inequality—especially racial, but also other forms of social disadvantage along lines of class, gender, sexuality, indigeneity, and geography. Its focus is the urban history of American cities, suburbs, and towns and how urban development since the advent of the motor-car suburb in the 1920s shaped American social life. Students will learn about and explore topics such as zoning, racial redlining and other forms of segregation, real estate markets, the home building industry, urban public policy and law, urban leisure, transportation, and many others. At the center of the course is the question of how people live in dense connectivity and community and what the history of the modern American city can tell us about the nature and durability of social inequality.

data.name: The Intellectual History of Black Women
data.description: This course will introduce students to the intellectual productions and theoretical traditions of African American women. Focused on the canonical texts of African American women, this class gestures toward diaspora as well. Moving chronologically from the history of slavery to the present will require that we simultaneously confront the question of what counts as “intellectual” history. Thus even as we will read the written words of black feminists across time, we will also call into question what Barbara Christian calls “the race for theory,” turning also to resistance practices, material culture, and bodily performance as sites of black feminist theorization.

data.name: From Medieval Bedlam to Prozac Nation: Intimate Histories of Psychiatry and Self
data.description: Humankind has long sought out keepers of its secrets and interpreters of its dreams: seers, priests, and, finally, psychiatrists. This lecture course will introduce students to the history of psychiatry in Europe, the United States, and beyond, from its pre-modern antecedents through the present day. Our focus will be on the long age of asylum psychiatry, but we will also consider the medical and social histories that intersect with, but are not contained by, asylum psychiatry: the rise of modern diagnostic systems, psychoanalysis, sexuality and stigma, race, eugenics, and pharmaceutical presents and futures.

data.name: Women, Gender and Feminism in Early Modern Italy (ITAL 1262)
data.description: Interested students must register for ITAL 1262.

data.name: The Persian Empire and Achaemenid Culture (CLAS 1230)
data.description: Interested students must register for CLAS 1230.

data.name: The Civil Rights Movement (AFRI 1090A)
data.description: Interested students must register for AFRI 1090A.

data.name: Legal History: The Case of the Middle East
data.description: This seminar is devoted to exploring questions of law and legal history in the Middle East. Together we shall navigate a range of imperial, colonial, and national legal systems in the region via select case studies from early modern to contemporary times. Major themes include not only Islamicate legal traditions and jurisdictions, but the intersections, tensions, and synergy between Islamic law and Roman, Persianate, and European imperial legacies; fellow Abrahamic faiths and the interplay between religion and secularism; and the evolving role of international law including human rights and humanitarian law. From early schools of Islamic jurisprudence to Ottoman, Egyptian, and Iranian constitutions, and the role of lawyers and judiciaries from decolonization to the Arab Spring uprisings, our goal is to explore the depth, diversity, and transformation of legal cultures and institutions across different epochs of Middle East history.

data.name: Thinking Historically: A History of History Writing
data.description: Philosopher George Santayana famously warned that “Those who cannot remember the past are condemned to repeat it.” Ten years later, industrialist Henry Ford perhaps even more famously dismissed that notion: “History is more or less bunk.” What we mean by history and how we construct and use it are essential questions in all societies. Thinking Historically explores how we view and employ the past. The course examines major ways of interpreting the past through a survey of historians and methods and studies how history is produced, used, and misused, by professionals as well as by the public.

data.name: On the Move: Problems and Histories of Tourism and Mobilities
data.description: This course examines histories of modern recreational tourism and the politics of mobility more generally. Tourism is a fascinating point of departure through which we can examine how histories of empire, consumerism, gender, and the environment all converge upon practices many (but certainly not all) of us take for granted. The premise of this seminar is based on two basic questions: through tourism, how do we change a place and how does that place change us? How does human mobility produce a new set of economic, social, and political relations? The answers will vary across time and space, but through engagement with scholarship and various primary sources we can come to better understand how the pleasure modern-day travelers derive from temporarily being someplace else is ultimately something deeply embedded into the structures and historical processes of the modern world itself.

data.name: Indigeneity and Assimilation in Southern Africa: The Khoe-San
data.description: South Africa is infamous for apartheid, the starkest form of settler colonialism. Yet 20th-century segregation reversed centuries of assimilationist policies against South Africa’s First Peoples: the Khoe-San. From the mid-17th century, this distinctive cultural group endured environmental and epidemiological shocks, conquest, and genocide. Their descendants only survived within a Dutch-speaking underclass. This seminar traces their survivance: from early encounters and violent subjugation to claims-making against 19th century British liberalism, ending with recent assertions of indigeneity among “Coloured” descendants in decolonizing South Africa. Khoe-San history—trauma, adaptation, and endurance—forms a crucial chapter in African history and comparative colonial history. P

data.name: North African History: 1800 to Present
data.description: This course focuses on the francophone Maghrib (Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia) and offers an introduction to major themes in the history of Africa and the Arab world in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Students will gain the tools to analyze and historicize the dynamic history of this region. We will examine a range of topics, including the transformations of pre-colonial social, economic and cultural patterns, conquest and resistance, comparative histories of colonialism, nationalism, decolonization and revolution, the consolidation of postcolonial states, regional cooperation, the rise of Islamism and civil conflicts, and the Arab Spring.

data.name: Making Revolutionary Cuba, 1959-Present
data.description: In January 1959, the forces of rebel leader Fidel Castro entered Havana and forever altered the destiny of their nation and world. This seminar will consider the six decades that followed the rise of a new revolutionary order, as well as its global repercussions. We will examine how political hegemony was achieved as well as the silences and exclusions on which it rested, with particular attention to ideology, race, sexuality, and culture.

data.name: America and the Middle East: Histories of Connection and Exchange
data.description: This seminar explores the history of connection and exchange between the diverse peoples and societies of two constructed regions: the Middle East and North America. Proceeding chronologically, we begin with the global context of Columbus’s fateful voyage in 1492, before turning to colonial Spanish, Portuguese, and English-British intersections with the Ottoman Empire and Morocco. While including Latin American, Caribbean, and Canadian case studies, the bulk of our course explores US ties to the “Mideast/MENA” region during the 19 th -21 st centuries. Yet, this isn’t a conventional course in diplomatic history. Rather, we’ll read closely for underlying socioeconomic, cultural, and intellectual processes—trade, migration, education, and evolving conceptions of race, religion, and citizenship—themes often overlooked by foreign relations, watershed events, or conflict. Our goal: to recognize how US-Middle East ties are far more complex, rich, and deep-rooted than is generally assumed.

data.name: Israel-Palestine: Lands and Peoples I
data.description: The Holocaust largely destroyed the centuries-long Jewish civilization in Europe. For Zionism, originating in late nineteenth-century antisemitism and East-Central European ethno-territorial nationalism, the “final solution” proved the need for a Jewish-majority state in Palestine. Yet the majority of the population in Eretz Israel was Palestinian. The creation of Israel in 1948 was the outcome of a bitter war with the local Arab population and the surrounding states, in the course of which most of the Palestinians were expelled or fled, facilitating the establishment of a Jewish-majority state. The seminar will discuss the fraught question of the two traumatic events of the Holocaust and the Nakba (the expulsion of 1948), and propose that we can both better understand these events, and begin the long path to reconciliation, by applying the tools of empathetic first-person history.

data.name: Early American and Atlantic Money
data.description: The history of finance has become a crucial site for studying governance and statecraft, for recovering the organizing logic of capitalism, and for recognizing the structures of power in any given society. Topics include the recurring debates over metallic and paper currencies, the emergence of a national banking systems, and the technologies of coinage, assaying, and counterfeiting. Particular focus on the relationship among finance, colonization, and slavery, as well as the many speculative monetary systems that circled around the Atlantic world from the fifteenth century through the nineteenth century.

data.name: Modern South Asia and the Image Archive
data.description: This seminar will look at how historians, art historians and artists have used an array of visual materials in the archive to make arguments about the past. We will read texts on the history of photography, on popular ephemera, from film studies, as well as interrogate the work of artists who draw on archives. How do we "read" images, interpret them, and how have they impacted our understanding of the past?

data.name: Environmental Pressures of South Asia
data.description: South Asia is one of the most densely populated geographical regions of the world. The traditional environmentalist view of separating/protecting ‘nature’ from human settlements often leads to conflicts of resources/land use here. This course studies the evolution of environmental governance in the diverse ecologies of South Asia across four substantive and methodological parts. Part I involves readings on environmental and agrarian systems in the Mughal Empire from the precolonial period (1526-1757) in South Asia. Part II involves readings on forestry laws from the British colonial period (1757-1947) in South Asia. Part III involves reviewing a series of books and films on environmental history in Asia. Part IV involves researching case studies focusing on local environment cultures from the postcolonial period (1947 to present) in South Asia. The course requires writing a final research paper based on a case study.

data.name: Modern Environmental Movements from Conservation to the Rights of Nature
data.description: What are the politics of creating and maintaining a livable Earth after industrialization? How has the environment figured in movements for equality and justice? This senior seminar examines environmental issues and responses under capitalism in North America and socialism in Russia and Eastern Europe. The course covers conservation and preservation; Indigenous anticolonial movements; responses to chemical and plastic pollution; anti-nuclear protest; environmental justice; climate change and anti-fossil fuel movements; and new forms of environmental organizing including the Rights of Nature movement and degrowth. Focused on the 19th and 20th centuries, the class will situate movements in ecological, political, and economic context, assess outcomes, and examine the relationships between economic form and politics. Using primary and secondary sources in a range of media, seminars will include short lectures, discussions, and student-led case studies that build toward a substantive final research project.

data.name: Planning the Family
data.description: This course explores 20th-century efforts to address population growth and family planning in a variety of global contexts, ranging from macro-level initiatives to individual practices. For some women, state leaders, and international experts, contraception meant sexual liberation, increased autonomy, and the reduction of economic hardship. But for others, the same biomedical technologies were associated with colonial control and efforts to reduce “undesirable” people. How is it possible for contraception to hold such expansive and contradictory meanings? In order to assess this central question, students will engage with critical theories of gender, race, class, nationalism, and decolonization.

data.name: Climates of Inequality: The Plantation-to-Petrochemical Complex
data.description: What is the relationship between land, labor, and global capitalist modernity? How does studying environmental injustice help resituate America’s colonial past and its imperial present? This interdisciplinary course introduces students to one of the most pressing environmental justice struggles in the nation today: Cancer Alley, USA. Together, we will explore the entwined histories of slavery, settler colonialism, and racial capitalism in Louisiana and the broader Gulf South, connecting colonial history to ongoing forms of state violence. Readings draw heavily from Environmental History, Native American and Indigenous Studies (NAIS), and Black Studies to address themes of ecological devastation and embodied violence, while also highlighting practices of resistance and resurgence from historical uprisings to contemporary cultural revitalization efforts. Students will learn to critically analyze how racialized systems of power and privilege structure inequality while envisioning alternative, more just futures.

data.name: Worlds on the Move:  Migration and Exchange Across Europe and the Mediterranean, 1492–1700
data.description: How did people travel across the Mediterranean in the early modern period? What documents and economic resources enabled their mobility? How did cities respond to migration, and what did it mean to cross borders in a world of empires, republics, and religious frontiers? This course explores the mechanisms and experiences of movement, examining how migration shaped the cultural, social, and political development of early modern societies. Drawing on the instructor’s expertise, the course places special emphasis on female mobility and the influence of gender relations on migratory experiences. Students will engage with various primary sources, such as archival documents, maps, iconographic materials, and literary texts, which will provide them with essential tools and methods for practicing history and deepen their understanding of how historians work.

data.name: History of Healing, Medicine, Technology, and Global Health in Africa
data.description: History of Healing, Medicine, Technology, and Global Health in Africa will examine the political, social, and cultural dimensions of health and healing across the African continent. Through historical case studies and contemporary debates, students will explore how indigenous knowledge, medical technologies, and public health interventions have shaped, and have been shaped by, Africans, colonialism, nationalist movements, and global health agendas. Students will be required to participate in a workshop and engage with primary and secondary sources to analyze the intersections of medicine, science, and technology and global health. Students will participate in seminar discussions and expert-led sessions, develop a research project that critically examines a historical or contemporary issue in African health and healing, and produce written reflections, reports, and oral presentations that connect historical insights to present-day challenges. The course emphasizes interdisciplinary thinking, historical analysis, and persuasive communication skills.

data.name: History Honors Workshop for Prospective Thesis Writers
data.description: Prospective honors students are encouraged to enroll in HIST 1992 during semesters 5 or 6. HIST 1992 offers a consideration of historical methodology and techniques of writing and research with the goal of preparing to write a senior thesis in history. The course helps students refine research skills, define a project, and prepare a thesis prospectus, which is required for admission to honors. Students who complete honors may count HIST 1992 as a concentration requirement. Limited to juniors who qualify for the honors program.

data.name: History Honors Workshop for Thesis Writers, Part I
data.description: All students admitted to the History Honors Program must enroll in HIST 1993 for two semesters of thesis research and writing. They may enroll in the course during semesters 6 and 7, or 7 and 8. Course work entails researching, organizing, writing a history honors thesis. Presentation of work and critique of peers' work required. Limited to seniors and juniors who have been admitted to History Honors Program. HIST 1993 is a mandatory S/NC course. See History Concentration Honors Requirements.

data.name: History Honors Workshop for Thesis Writers, Part II
data.description: This is the second half of a year-long course, upon completion the grade will revert to HIST 1993. Prerequisite: HIST 1993.

data.name: The Roots of History
data.description: “The Roots of History” encourages critical thinking about some of the different ways in which historians approach thinking and writing about the past. In particular, we will explore some of the major theoretical stances that have influenced the discipline of history. Our focus throughout will be the interplay between theory and practice. By examining how historians have grappled with questions posed by influential thinkers (often working within other fields of knowledge), we will chart the trajectory of the discipline and assess its working methods. Required for all first-year PhD students in History.

data.name: The Practice of History
data.description: This graduate seminar is about the professional skills, tools, and careers of historians—from hallmark best practices to new directions. We will engage different kinds of careers historians are pursuing today, the practical skills and resources necessary to meet them, and how historians are deploying those skills in multiple professional arenas, both in and beyond the academy. We shall also discuss some of the prevalent (although often unwritten) social and cultural norms of academia and university life to prepare you to navigate your training, career, and various institutional contexts that accompany and follow completion of a Ph.D. in History

data.name: Prospectus Development Seminar
data.description: This collaborative seminar focuses on identifying and contextualizing a dissertation topic; choosing a dissertation committee and a support network; designing and executing plans for research and writing; and articulating a thesis project as a prospectus, grant proposal, and other oral and written forms. This is a required course for and open only to third-year students in the History Ph.D. program.

data.name: Rethinking the Civil Rights Movement
data.description: This graduate course encourages a rethinking of the complex components, arguments and activities that have characterized what we have come to know as the Civil Rights Movement, concentrating primarily on African American agency, actions and politics, through careful reading of recent scholarship in the field. While knowledge of U.S. history is preferred, this course asks larger thematic questions about protest movements (the role of the state, relationships with and between oppressed groups and organizations, and periodization), that will interest non-Americanists also. Some of the topics covered include: gender, organizing and strategies, the local, global ramifications and interactions, organizational structures and politics, and the recent concept of the Long Civil Rights Movement. M

data.name: Latin American Historiography
data.description: This course examines the development of historical writings on Latin America produced in the United States from the late nineteenth century until the present. We will focus on themes, such as race, gender, labor, subaltern studies, dependency theory, postcolonial analysis, and post-modernism, to understand the diverse approaches to Latin American history. M

data.name: Orientalism
data.description: Europe’s fascination with “the Orient” generated a major tradition of intellectual, literary, and artistic production, beginning in the medieval era and persisting to the present day. This seminar explores the social and intellectual history of Orientalism, considering the ways that it has intersected with the study of power, race, sexuality, religion, and language. Readings to include Edward Said’s seminal work on this subject alongside several critiques, correctives, and debates that have emerged in recent years.

data.name: Perspectives on Jewish History
data.description: Jewish history has a complex dynamic. The development of Jewish society is fed not only by Jewish tradition but also by developments in non-Jewish society. Equally, the place of Jews in the non-Jewish world is determined not only by the attitudes of non-Jews but also by the economic, social, and political agency wielded by Jews themselves. This graduate seminar will, in a broad chronological framework, examine the complicated interplay of forces which shaped both the Jewish experience in the non-Jewish world and the non-Jewish world’s treatment of its Jewish minority. Among the topics to be discussed: the places of Jews in premodern Christian and Muslim societies; Jewish early modernity; the historical trajectory of Jew-hate; changing Jewish responses to non-Jewish culture; Jewish power, powerlessness, and political development; the modern Jewish experience before, during, and after the Holocaust.

data.name: Thesis Preparation
data.description: For graduate students who have met the residency requirement and are continuing research on a full time basis.

data.name: History Dissertation Writing Workshop
data.description: This seminar is for History PhD students in residence at Brown who are post-exams and are at any stage of writing their dissertations. It is intended to support dissertators by providing a structured community for setting and sharing goals, writing, and discussing chapters-in-progress. It meets every other week and is a half credit S/NC course.

data.name: Environment, Violence, Repair
data.description: Recent conversations in environmental justice foreground harm and repair, as the scope of environmental questions expands to include exposure, toxicity, war, and infrastructures. This seminar positions violence and repair as entry-points to an ethnographic understanding of environmental harm, disturbance, and change through an interdisciplinary body of work that includes environmental history and film. The course connects recent ethnographic accounts of exposure, extraction, and pollution to critiques of violence, imperialism, and statecraft, and asks about the possibilities and limitations of repair, refusal, and survival. Reading critical theory on violence next to environmental degradation opens up space to think and learn about how colonial and state projects terra/aqua-form the environment and lifeways and calls us to think critically about environmental struggles from the perspective of resistance, repair, and non-western and non-secular responses to disaster, disruption, and change.

data.name: The Cogut Institute for the Humanities Research Seminar
data.description: This yearlong seminar involves reading and discussing in-progress research by the annual fellows of the Cogut Institute for the Humanities, an interdisciplinary group of faculty, postdoctoral fellows, graduate students, and undergraduates engaged in extended research on a major project or honors thesis. Students read a wide range of works-in-progress, prepare questions and participate in seminar discussions, serve as first questioners for specific sessions assigned to them in advance, and present their own work twice during the year. Students are required to complete both semesters (HMAN 1000A in the fall and HMAN 1000B in the spring) to receive credit. Admission to the course requires that students have received the Cogut Institute Undergraduate Fellowship for the year in which they enroll. More information about the fellowship and application process may be found at https://humanities.brown.edu/apply/undergraduate-fellowship.

data.name: We the People
data.description: How do individual people come together to make a People? We will read key ancient texts including Aristotle, Livy, and Cicero, enlightenment thinkers like Rousseau and Hobbes, and modern theorists from Arendt and Schmitt to Chakrabarty and Mouffe to see how the “we” of We the People has been used both to include and exclude. We will also consider practices of political action both ancient and modern. Can a People create itself by a vote, a manifesto, or a riot? What is the relationship between the People and populism? The course is explicitly grounded in the ancient world; as well as analyzing how ancient concepts have echoed through the centuries, we will also look to the distant past for difference, asking how we might propose alternative genealogies of the People.

data.name: Settler Colonial Cities: Indigenous Histories, Presents, and Futures
data.description: The course examines the Indigenous histories, presents, and futures of settler colonial cities. Founded on Indigenous lands expropriated for town building, modern cities in settler colonial societies were and are complicit in processes displacing Indigenous people and appropriating their cultures, objects, and ancestors. We will draw on anthropology, archaeology, archival history, geography, heritage preservation, public art, and urban studies to explore colonialist tactics of spatial removal and symbolic and documentary violence aimed at erasure. Most importantly, we will look at how Indigenous people have challenged claims of their absence in urbanizing landscapes through their oral histories, writing, and material culture, and by their presence and diverse experiences in cities in the United States, Canada, Australia, and beyond.

data.name: The Politics of Health and Disease (POLS 1295)
data.description: Interested students must register for POLS 1295.

data.name: Bottom Ecologies: Blackness, Queerness, and the Environment
data.description: How have Black queer communities — and minoritarian communities more broadly — transformed environments of shame and disposability into sources of erotic power, pleasure, and collective survival? Building on Toni Morrison’s “Sula” and theories from Black feminisms, queer-of-color critique, and Black ecological thought, this course views the “bottom” as Black queer ecological thought. We will trace how Black queer communities generate counter-knowledge, reshape racialized and sexual subjectivities, and challenge conventional environmental discourse by queering ecological analysis. Ultimately, this course examines the “bottom” as both a sexual position and an ecological site historically raced, and marked as disposable, dirty, abject. Readings from Morrison, Jennifer Nash, Audre Lorde, Darieck Scott, Juana María Rodríguez, Kathryn Bond Stockton, and Leo Bersani will guide our exploration of how landscapes of racial abjection and erotic desire converge, offering new critical tools for environmental humanities.
