data.name: Introduction to Digital Humanities
data.description: Digital humanities uses digital methodologies and formats to answer humanities research questions, produce and share knowledge, and teach. It encompasses critical studies of digital environments, innovative modes of researching and advancing arguments (including methodologies for constituting archives, analyzing texts and images, and visualizing data), new forms of scholarly and general publications, and digital pedagogy. The digital changes the way we research, understand, and share information in the humanities. This course will introduce students to a range of methodologies and critical lenses. Students will learn to collect resources formatted for machine-actionable research, analyze qualitative and quantitative datasets, and visualize and present their findings. They will consider the ethics of digital work and publication. Each student will plan a digital humanities project. This course is open to all graduate students and fulfills a requirement for the doctoral certificate in digital humanities.

data.name: Digital Humanities Capstone Seminar
data.description: Participants in the Digital Humanities Capstone Seminar will learn how to design and build their own digital humanities project and/or participate as a key member on an already established digital humanities project with a faculty leader through hands-on, step by step instruction. Students will grapple with key methodologies and theoretical frameworks (including critical, Black, Indigenous, and Latinx digital humanities) and consider how to situate their own work within these ideas. Seminar participants will participate in the Digital Humanities Salon, where they will also present their project. Where desired and advisable, the capstone project may build toward dissertation work. Admission in the course is conditional on enrollment in the doctoral certificate in digital humanities, and partial or full completion of the digital tools and methods requirement.

data.name: Digital Humanities Capstone Seminar
data.description: Participants in the Digital Humanities Capstone Seminar will learn how to design and build their own digital humanities project and/or participate as a key member on an already established digital humanities project with a faculty leader through hands-on, step by step instruction. Students will grapple with key methodologies and theoretical frameworks (including critical, Black, Indigenous, and Latinx digital humanities) and consider how to situate their own work within these ideas. Where desired and advisable, the capstone project may build toward dissertation work. Admission in the course is conditional on enrollment in the doctoral certificate in digital humanities, and partial or full completion of the digital tools and methods requirement.

data.name: Artists and the East India Company (HIAA 2780)
data.description: Interested students must register for HIAA 2780.

data.name: Race and its Avatars: Literary Identity Politics From the Medieval to the Present (ENGL 2762A)
data.description: Interested students must register for ENGL 2762A.

data.name: From Big Data to Lilliputians: Considerations of Scale Across the Disciplines
data.description: We live in an era of big problems and small details. It is a time of globalization and atomization, of big data and nanotechnology—nothing is too minute to escape surveillance, regulation, or sale, and scalability is often used as a measure of success. Scale is the stuff of awe and the sublime and the feeling of utter insignificance. Can one person’s behavior change the way an entire system works (“personalized medicine,” or climate change)? When does a change in scale mean a change in kind? Is morality/ethics a question of scale? Should we think big, or is the devil in the details? We will discuss the theme of “Scale” from a variety of historical and other perspectives with texts from philosophy, literature, history of science, theology, psychology, sociology, and political theory, as well as film and poetry.

data.name: Approaches to Environmental Humanities Research
data.description: This collaborative, interdisciplinary seminar engages modes of inquiry in environmental humanities research and accounts for a range of theories and methods that encompass qualitative, historical, decolonial, and community-engaged scholarships, among others. The seminar focuses in particular on human and nonhuman relations and their impacts on environmental change, drawing on concepts and practices such as survivance, duration, weathering, extraction, and geological time. In addition to scholarly and theoretical texts, we will explore films, novels, creative nonfiction, and performances. Assignments help students engage and refine approaches to environmental scholarly inquiry and practice in a time of intensifying planetary and local crisis.

data.name: Project Development Workshop
data.description: In this capstone course, students completing the Doctoral Certificate in Collaborative Humanities pursue individual or collaborative projects, such as a dissertation chapter, an article on method/theory, or a stand-alone essay related to the larger field. The workshop provides a collaborative and supportive space in which students from different disciplines can share their work and receive and give feedback that will broaden and sharpen the framing of their projects. At the end of the semester, participants present in a Collaborative Public Workshop. Admission to the seminar requires a formal application process and the completion of two HMAN 2400 seminars.

data.name: The Order of Evils: Phenomenological, Moral, and Political Investigations
data.description: A critical reading of "The Order of Evils: Toward an Ontology of Morals" (2000), a “poststructuralist” moral treatise that combines phenomenological and structural analysis and is attuned to the historicity of knowledge and moral matters. The book understands evil as discrete harm, which becomes a moral matter only once it is judged as gratuitous, and an object of critique only once it is grasped in its regularity. The seminar, oriented toward revising the book, reexamines the reasoning that supports these intuitions and their implications: a transformation of moral discourse; a new ontology of “moral things”; the primacy of others over the self; a downgrade of “liberty,” “justice,” and “rights”; and a critical interest in the historical conditions of political action. Seminar participants will also read works by thinkers who inspired the project, including Kant, Marx, Heidegger, Levinas, Lyotard, and Derrida.

data.name: Beginning Hindi or Urdu
data.description: Introduces conversation, reading, and writing of modern standard Hindi and the Devanagari script. Those who already know Devanagari but have rusty conversation skills may join the class second semester; obtain instructor's permission during the first semester. Those who prefer to learn Urdu and the Persian script should contact the instructor. Prerequisite: HNDI 0100.

data.name: Intermediate Hindi-Urdu
data.description: A continuation of HNDI 0100-0200. Introduces the variation of the Persian script used for Urdu. Prepares students to communicate in written and spoken language. Activities are conducted in Hindi/Urdu. Meets four hours weekly. Prerequisite: HNDI 0300.

data.name: Advanced Hindi-Urdu
data.description: Each student follows an independent reading list determined in consultation with the instructor. The readings may include folk tales, journalistic prose, 20th-century literature, classical Urdu poetry of the 17th to 19th centuries, or subjects in nonfiction. The class meets together three hours weekly for discussion. Each student also spends one hour weekly with the instructor. Prerequisite: HNDI 0400.

data.name: Costs of War
data.description: This interdisciplinary seminar explores a ground-level view of war and its human, economic, environmental, and social consequences. We primarily take up the example of the post-9/11 wars, focusing less on the causes of war, on battles, elite war strategies, or relations between states than on what war is like for those caught within it, what larger social and economic contexts make it more or less likely, what are its impacts for many differently situated peoples, and how myths about violence, and even the category of war itself, often obscures fuller understanding.

data.name: Reimagining Human Security
data.description: The concept of human security– in both theory and practice– has significantly evolved over the past two decades, with the idea that people, and the protection of human life and dignity, should be the central focus of governmental and intergovernmental security policy and practice. Yet, despite many advances in the field, the world is more dangerous than ever, and vulnerable civilians are increasingly threatened by a multitude of challenges including war, climate change, pandemics, natural disasters, the erosion of humanitarian and human rights law, and rapid advances in world-changing technologies. How do global issues such as climate change, pandemics, artificial intelligence, and migration impact human security? In what ways do social, economic, environmental, and political factors intersect in the context of human security? What ethical considerations should be considered when reimagining approaches to human security?

data.name: National Security Decision Making
data.description: Why do world leaders sometimes choose the foreign policies that end disastrously? This undergraduate lecture examines theories of individual and group decision-making in the context of national security policy. It connects analytical perspectives from political science, social psychology, and organizational theory with a wide variety of historical cases featuring the United States, Soviet Union, China, India, Pakistan, Iraq, and Vietnam. What constitutes an “effective” decision-making process? What types of individuals, groups and institutions foster more effective processes? When are individuals or groups prone towards risky decisions? Do decision-making processes matter in “high-stakes” issues, such as war and peace? The last third of the course will examine these questions in the context of the 2018 US-China Trade War.

data.name: Foundations of Development
data.description: This course presents an interdisciplinary approach to the study of development. The course examines what constitutes development from a variety of different disciplinary perspectives, and the course examines how and in what context the term “development” itself has evolved over time. The goal of this course is to provide students an intellectual and conceptual grounding for study a variety of issues surrounding development, whether in the global North or South.

data.name: Foundations of Security
data.description: This course introduces students to contemporary international security topics with a special focus on problems associated with war and violent conflict in the 21st century. It mixes the theoretical, historical, analytical, and practical. Students learn theory on the causes of war, how wars are fought, civil-military relations, the costs and consequences of warfare, and more. They also learn about the organization of the U.S. and other national security establishments. Using these tools, we will apply social science methods and critical thinking to security “hot spots” and wars around the world. There will be an emphasis on analyzing the unfolding war in Ukraine, as well as the prospects for U.S.-China rivalry and/or military conflict in the future, including within a Taiwan scenario. Aside from readings, lectures and discussions, students will benefit from a variety of films, simulations, walking excursions, and debates.

data.name: Nonprofit Organizations
data.description: Contemporary nonprofits and their role in community building and shaping public policy are central to this course. Topics include how strong coalitions impact housing, welfare and children's policy, organizing empowered communities, the influential and engaged donor and building the value of nonprofits. Case studies will be featured and new nonprofit models will be conceptualized to strategically address critical human need. Enrollment limited to 20 juniors, seniors, and graduate students concentrating in Public Policy. This course satisfies the American Institutions requirement.

data.name: Research Design and Methods
data.description: This course provides an introduction to research design in the social sciences. The goals of the course are two-fold: to instruct students to critically analyze scholarly articles in terms of research design and to guide students in designing an original research project. The methods presented in the course include qualitative methods (e.g., interviews, focus groups, participant observation, and process tracing) and quantitative methods (e.g., surveys, observational studies, and experiments). The course is designed as preparation for a senior thesis or original research project. All assignments in the course revolve around the development of an original research project. The research itself will not be conducted as part of the course. There are no prerequisites for the course and a background in statistics is not required.

data.name: Energy, Climate and Society
data.description: Energy shapes society, from the daily lives of local communities to patterns of global inequality. In this course, we explore the social dimensions of energy production and consumption. We start by examining fossil fuels and their wide-ranging effects on communities and the environment, then shift to the ongoing energy transitions and the social forces that help or hinder the large-scale adoption of renewables. Throughout the course, we will ask who benefits and who bears the costs of energy production and transition, and focus on understanding the equity implications of these changes and the prospects for a sustainable society. Students will develop policy analysis skills by identifying current energy challenges, assessing their social impacts, and proposing evidence-based solutions.

data.name: Contemporary Approaches to International Security Research
data.description: This seminar surveys a variety of contemporary scholarship—not classic texts—related to international security. We will critically engage recent arguments that draw upon a range of theoretical and empirical approaches to explain the determinants and consequences of war and peace. The course begins with a relatively cursory introduction to political science theory, design, and empirics. Each subsequent week investigates a particular area of scholarly attention, introducing students to a handful of recent contributions. Class time will be used to provide short lectures that provide background on each issue area, as well as for broader group discussion. The course will expose students to the ‘cutting edge’ in international security research, and provide the analytical tools to evaluate and contribute themselves.

data.name: Mexican Migration and US Immigration Policy
data.description: This course traces the history of Mexican migration and its relationship to US immigration policy. It uses Mexican migration as a case study—a lens through which to engage key topics in the study of migration, such as the construction of borders, the historical continency of legality, the social determinants of migratory departure, and the impacts of migration on sending and receiving communities. This course shifts between history and the present moment to develop a layered understanding of migration and to contemplate how immigration policy has functioned in the past and may function in the future. It centers the experiences of migrants and their families as they navigate leaving home and building lives in a country simultaneously reliant on and hostile to their presence, offering insight into a complicated and shifting reality affecting millions on both sides of the border.

data.name: Public Opinion, Social Media, and Politics
data.description: This course explores the role of public opinion, introducing the dynamics of citizens’ social and political attitudes, especially focusing on the intersection of social media and politics. This course will introduce a multitude of topics on the political consequences of social media use including political participation, polarization, emotional well-being, and campaign advertisements. Theoretically, we will dive into understanding the classical theories in public opinion and extend to applications of how and why social media influence political behavior and attitudes. Empirically, the course will focus on practical, hands-on experience with analyzing real-world data, primarily using pre-existing datasets, surveys, and tools for data analysis, to gain insight into social science research.

data.name: Crime and Punishment in Global Perspective
data.description: Why do we believe crimes deserve punishment? In this course, we’ll explore how societies around the world criminalize people, activities, and substances to justify the punishment of transgressions. Each week, we’ll examine a case from a different country, such as abortion in Mexico, evidence fabrication in India, organized crime in the United States, mass incarceration in Brazil, or police violence in France. We’ll watch international films, listen to podcasts, and read across disciplines like anthropology, law, sociology, and political science to understand how criminalization affects people’s lives and question the philosophical reasons behind punishment. You will leave the course with an analytical toolkit to rethink justice systems and develop innovative alternatives.

data.name: What Makes Policies Work?
data.description: What happens after laws and policies are enacted? What makes policy work in practice? What does it take – in terms of policy designs, resources, organizations, politics, expertise, and more – to turn formal rules into lived realities? How might this this differ across populations, over time, and over different geographic spaces? This course will take up these puzzles by examining multiple aspects of policymaking -- mobilization, policy designs, policy enactment, implementation, outcomes – across policy domains including k-12 education, health policy, social policy, elections policy, and more. Class members will have opportunities to examine the development and experience of policy in other domains through their independent research projects. This course is designed as a Junior seminar for the IAPA concentration and is open to IAPA and Political Science concentrators.

data.name: Diplomacy and Development: Related but Different Missions
data.description: This course will explore two international relations missions—diplomacy and development cooperation-- that complement one another but differ in substance and nature. Diplomacy, comprised of statecraft, policy formulation, representation, reporting and negotiation, will be treated in opening segments. The diplomatic mission is about influencing other sovereign states to bridge national differences in an effort to promote interests and avoid conflict. The course will explore these aspects using simulations, including a mock negotiation, a congressional hearing, and other pedagogical approaches.

data.name: Diplomacy and Development: Related but Different Missions
data.description: This course will explore two international relations missions—diplomacy and development cooperation-- that complement one another but differ in substance and nature. Diplomacy, comprised of statecraft, policy formulation, representation, reporting and negotiation, will be treated in opening segments. The diplomatic mission is about influencing other sovereign states to bridge national differences in an effort to promote interests and avoid conflict. The course will explore these aspects using simulations, including a mock negotiation, a congressional hearing, and other pedagogical approaches.

data.name: Inequality and Social Mobility in America
data.description: Income and wealth inequality in America are at their highest levels in a century. Historically, one reason Americans have been thought to tolerate inequality is that we tend to believe that our society is a mobile one, where people can easily move from one social class to another. But in recent years, inequality and stagnant social mobility have been associated with increasing social and political distrust and unrest. In this junior seminar, we will examine the economic and political, and ideological factors that have contributed to this historical moment, learn about how Americans experience living in an unequal society, and consider the future of equality and social mobility in the United States. How should our society be shaped, and what are our obligations as members of that society?

data.name: Urban Ethnography and Public Policy
data.description: Ethnography is a social science method in which the researcher participates in and closely observes the lives of others — getting, as Erving Goffman once put it, “close to them while they are responding to what life does to them.” Since the early twentieth century, urban ethnographers have used this approach to understand how city life “works,” revealing how people produce, interpret, and respond to the social forces that shape everyday urban experience. This course uses ethnographic inquiry to examine how urban policy intersects with individual lives. How are large-scale programs and institutions created, implemented, and experienced through daily encounters between residents, service providers, and public institutions?

data.name: Introduction to Corporate Law and Public Policy
data.description: This course will give students an introduction to business organizations – the law that governs corporations and partnerships, how they raise money in the financial markets, and to explore the public policy issues that inform the regulation of business and finance. We will look at business organizations, law that governs how companies raise money, operation of the stock markets, insider trading, and the regulation of institutional investors including mutual funds, hedge funds and private equity funds. We will finish by taking up corporations as persons, their social obligations and the recent Supreme Court cases on corporations and the First Amendment. Overrides granted based on random drawing for 11 seniors and 8 juniors; to be eligible you must turn in the first week’s assignment and attend the first class. Put course in your cart to access the assignment via canvas.

data.name: Human Rights and AI: Impacts, Risks, and Opportunities
data.description: Technology is not magic—it's power, coded and deployed. Artificial intelligence is becoming one of the most powerful forces shaping our future, determining who has access to opportunity, whose voices are heard, and how resources and rights are distributed. The Human Rights and AI seminar explores the complex, rapidly evolving landscape of AI and its profound impacts on human rights at the intersection of technology, public policy, and international law. Will AI hollow out our civil and human rights through invisible, automated systems that surveil, sort, and silence? Can AI serve liberation and justice if shaped, designed, and deployed from a rights-respecting framework? We will critically analyze both the harms and opportunities that AI poses to fundamental rights, and if civil and human rights laws and frameworks are relevant to responsible AI governance.

data.name: International Security Seminar:  Dynamics of Great Power Rivalry
data.description: This course examines the most dangerous phenomenon in contemporary international relation today: great power rivalry. We will investigate the main drivers of great power rivalry, its manifestations across varying geographic and functional domains, as well possible methods for mitigating rivalry. While discussions will touch on middle powers and alternative great powers, the focus will be on U.S. relations with both Russia and also China. Special attention is given to the various flashpoints in these relationships, whether the on-going Ukraine War or the South China Sea and also Taiwan. Rivalry dynamics will be assessed at all the various levels of intensity, ranging from influence operations to hybrid conflicts to limited wars and even to nuclear operations. Special attention will be paid to the costs of war, as well as to ways to restrain rivalry, including dialogue, institutions, and arms control.

data.name: The Politics of Regulation: Technology, Finance, and Climate Change
data.description: Technological innovations are a central force for economic development, but today we are facing some of their most severe and unintended consequences. Some observers argue that only market competition can enable the blossoming of human ingenuity, while others argue that state planning is essential for controlling the dangers inherent in new technologies. Rather than extol or deny the benefits that can come from technology, this course examines how governments in different historical circumstances have attempted to shape and regulate three of the most challenging risks facing advanced societies.

data.name: Iran and the Islamic Revolution
data.description: Shattering events of 1978-80 in Iran unfolded against the backdrop of the previous decades of Iranian history, knowing that history is essential to understanding the revolution. The revolution cannot be appreciated without studying the enormous effects it's had over the last 35 years. This course places the anti-Shah movement and the rise of religious power in the context of Iran's century of modern history. We conclude by focusing on today's Iran, the upheaval following the 2009 election, reformist president election in 2013, and prospects for reconciliation with the US. Enrollment limited to 20 juniors & seniors. Priority given to IR seniors.

data.name: Overcoming Threats to Human Security
data.description: This course provides students with a comprehensive introduction to exploring challenges and opportunities related to global challenges from both a human and national security perspective – with a special focus on putting people and communities, as opposed to national interests, at the center of attention. Students will gain a deep understanding of key issues including humanitarian crises caused by natural disasters, and the impacts of climate change, food and water security, urbanization, mass migration, and infectious disease/pandemics on vulnerable people around the world.

data.name: State and Nation Building in Communist and Post Communist Europe
data.description: This seminar investigates the politics of state-building and nation-building in communist and post-communist Eastern Europe since World War I. The course is designed as a Senior Seminar for the IAPA concentration, and its major requirement is the writing of a 25-to-30-page research paper on a subject of the student’s choosing (Capstone Project). Ideally, students will come to the course with some idea of a problem or question that the want to spend the better part of the semester researching. The seminar, however, is open anybody with a demonstrated interest in the region and who is ready to read and seriously discuss texts drawn from a wide range of vocations –from literature and politics, to history, philosophy, economics, sociology, political science, and public policy.

data.name: Politics of Reproductive Justice
data.description: Catherine MacKinnon once said “feminism has no theory of the state.” She could also have said “scholars of the state have no theory of feminism.” Thankfully times have changed. In this class, we will aim to understand why we need to think about states and gender together. We will investigate this intersection by focusing on a pressing contemporary issue: reproductive rights. Throughout the course, we will think through several questions. Why do states govern reproductive decisions and what strategies do state officials use? Does state intervention in reproduction reflect assumptions about masculinity and femininity? Does state intervention exacerbate or attenuate gendered differences and inequalities? How do organized interest groups challenge or alter state policies? We will read literature from around the world, while paying special attention to the American case.

data.name: Contemporary Digital Policy and Politics
data.description: This course will examine the politics and processes for making policies related to the internet and digital policy issues. We will examine current issues at the national level, including the White House and federal agencies, Congress, international institutions and industry on issues such as privacy and information security, and on debates like whether and how to regulate Big Tech. Topics covered include the creation of national policies at the White House, the regulatory process, legislation, standards, global implications and the politics of technological change.

data.name: Senior Honors Thesis
data.description: Open only to Senior students accepted into the honors program. Instructor permission required. This course is the second of the two required courses for students writing a thesis in International and Public Affairs, Development Studies, International Relations, or Public Policy. It is taught as an independent study.

data.name: Mental Health Among Rohingya Displaced Children and Adolescents
Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation (HSCT) and Global Health
Accelerated Elementary Italian
data.description: This is an intensive elementary language course designed for students with no previous knowledge of Italian. Knowledge of another Romance language, although not required, is recommended. The course develops basic communication skills in Italian and explores practices, products, and perspectives of Italian culture. Four meetings a week plus one conversation hour. This course is ideal for students interested in fast-tracking their language learning to meet Brown-in-Bologna requirements. Enrollment is limited to 15. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.

data.name: Elementary Italian
data.description: See Elementary Italian (ITAL 0100) for course description.

data.name: Elementary Italian
data.description: See Elementary Italian (ITAL 0100) for course description.

data.name: Intermediate Italian II
data.description: Review of specific grammar problems. Reading of one novel and newspaper articles. Compositions and oral presentations. Three Italian films. Prerequisite: ITAL 0300, or placement by examination.

data.name: Advanced Italian II
data.description: A sixth semester course with intensive practice in speaking and writing. Short stories, poems, music, and movies will be used to discuss Italian Society from the Second World War through the present. We will explore some important themes--family, religion, gender, and politics. Class discussion, compositions, oral presentations, and a final paper. Prerequisite: ITAL 0500, placement by examination.

data.name: Opera: Italy Onstage and Backstage
data.description: Opera is one of Italy’s most enduring contributions to the cultural world – but just exactly what is opera? How does it work and how has it been used throughout history to propose, support, and undermine ideas about topics as broad as nationalism, race, gender, and class? Through a focus on some of the most influential operas written in Italian, we will consider how this unique art form is both in dialogue with itself and the world around it. This course has no prerequisites and does not require prior knowledge of music nor opera.

data.name: Intensive Language and Culture Proseminar
data.description: The Intensive Language and Culture Proseminar is a mandatory course designed to prepare students enrolled in the Brown-in-Bologna program to function well within the academic and social context of Bologna and to deepen their knowledge of the political, cultural and social environment in which they will be living for the duration of the semester. The Proseminar includes three components: (1) Intensive Language and Culture; (2) Bologna hidden town; (3) a number of activities organized by the program throughout the semester and a number of individual mandatory activities. At the end of the Proseminar course students will attain an in-depth and experiential knowledge of the Italian society.

data.name: Cinema Neorealista Italiano
data.description: According to Pier Paolo Pasolini, Italian neorealism “represented the first act of critical consciousness, politically and ideologically, that Italy had of itself.” This course aims to show some of the classic neorealist films made in Italy in the 1940s: from Luchino Visconti’s Ossessione to Roberto Rossellini’s Germania anno zero and Vittorio De Sica’s Umberto D., neorealist cinema captures Italy’s new gaze onto itself, onto its hardships and its hopes. This course will be taught in Italian.

data.name: Great Authors and Works of Italian Renaissance: Ariosto’s Orlando furioso
data.description: This class will read the most successful narrative poem of the Early Modern European period, Ariosto’s Orlando furioso (“The Madness of Orlando,” 1516-32), a work so popular it increased European literacy and rivaled the Bible in editions published. It combined the genres of chivalric romance, the crusading epic, and the classic heroic epic, but features ladies-errant along with knights and shrewdly satirizes hypermasculine late feudalism (the philosopher Hegel claimed it had destroyed chivalry). It helped drive Cervantes’ Don Quixote mad, fueled a fierce debate in relation to Tasso’s crusade epic Jerusalem Delivered, and in England influenced Sidney, Spenser, Shakespeare, Lord Byron, and Virginia Woolf (Orlando, 1928). Depending on enrollment, we will read/discuss in Italian and/or English. Readers in English will field-test a new translation by Ariosto scholar Albert Ascoli to be published by Oxford University Press.

data.name: Medicine and Disability in Shakespeare and Early Modern Drama (COLT 1411F)
data.description: Interested students must register for COLT 1411F.

data.name: Cultures of Fascism
data.description: It has often been stated that fascism, as a totalitarian political system, could not produce a culture of its own but only propaganda or at best ideology. In this course we will explore this proposition by exploring some of fascism’s products in texts, film, art and architecture through the lens of critics and theorists of fascism such as Arendt, Benjamin, Kracauer, Gramsci, Bataille, Primo Levi (among others). Our focus will be on the Italian and German examples.

data.name: Independent Study Project (Undergraduate)
data.description: Undergraduate Independent Study supervised by a member of the Italian Studies Faculty. Students may pursue independent research in order to prepare for their honors thesis or honors multimedia project, or they may enroll in the course in order to work individually with a faculty member on a specific area of Italian Studies not covered in the current course offerings. Section numbers vary by instructor. Please check Banner for the correct section number and CRN to use when registering for this course.

data.name: Worlds on the Move: Migration & Exchange Across Europe and the Mediterranean 1492–1700 (HIST 1978K)
data.description: Interested students must register for HIST1978K.

data.name: Microhistory
data.description: Microhistory emerged in the 1970s in Italy, but was quickly embraced by scholars across the globe. Microhistory questioned totalizing explanations of historical change; rejected anachronism in all forms; and recovered the voices of individuals left out of traditional historical narratives. This course explores and critiques the method. Participants write an article in their area of interest, informed by microhistory. The course is taught in English.

data.name: Emergence of the Vernacular: Prestige and Critique (Spanish & Italian)
data.description: Using pertinent sources drawn primarily from Latin, Spanish, Italian, and their early variants, this class will study the emergence and development of the vernacular from its beginnings through the Early Modern period. We will consider theorizations of vernacularity alongside some of its most pressing debates as they circulated throughout Spain, Italy, and the Americas, in particular. To this end, we will examine historical sources that exemplify the wide reach of the sociopolitical, linguistic, and identitary anxieties and violences surrounding linguistic development, while concentrating our attentions on literary sources (prose, drama, poetry) and didactic manuals and epistles that directly comment upon the status of language(s), vilifying, defending, redefining, or reaffirming them. Students should be proficient in either Spanish or Italian, and have advanced reading abilities in the other.

data.name: Italian Studies Colloquium
data.description: The Italian Studies Colloquium is a forum for an exchange of ideas and work of the community of Italian scholars at Brown and invited outside scholars. Graduate students present their work in progress, and engage the work of faculty and visitors. They are expected to come prepared with informed questions on the topic presented. Presentations in both Italian and English. Instructor permission required.

data.name: Japanese Language Through Popular Culture
data.description: Japanese pop culture has been disseminated worldwide and it is one of the top reasons why people start studying Japanese today. This content-based language course examines various aspects of Japanese pop culture including but not limited to manga, anime, and video games. While students discuss topics related to Japanese pop culture, they will also learn grammar structures that enable them to write formal essays in Japanese. Through the materials of Japanese pop culture, students will also develop the skills needed to succeed in today’s world, such as critical thinking, problem solving, creativity, digital literacy, and communication. Above all, this course will emphasize creativity and help students learn how to attract people.

data.name: Jesus (RELS 0014)
data.description: Interested students must register for RELS 0014.

data.name: Happiness and the Pursuit of the Good Life (RELS 0010)
data.description: Interested students must register for RELS 0010.

data.name: American Jewish History from the Great Migration to the  Contemporary Resurgence of Antisemitism
data.description: In this course we will explore the forces that have shaped American Jewry and made it what it is today.  We will ask how the Jews who came to America as immigrants created a unique form of Jewish life that combined the political and social traditions of their old countries with the ethos and values they found here.  We will explore the place of American Jews in the Jewish world prior to World War II and how this affected the American Jewish public arena.  Our examination of the post-Holocaust years will focus on the creation of a new American Judaism, as well as the cultural, literary, and ideological accomplishments of American Jews.  Finally, we will discuss the development of antisemitism in the US from the 1920s to its contemporary resurgence.

data.name: Race, Sexuality, and the Jews
data.description: This seminar offers an interdisciplinary and intersectional examination of the historical and contemporary experiences of Jews from antiquity through the present. We will learn about social and cultural constructions of race, ethnicity, gender, and sexuality, focusing on Jews and other neighboring minority and majority communities; we will study how in different geographical and temporal spheres these communities have defined or blurred boundaries, shaping both distinctly visible and invisible characteristics; we will examine how perceptions of race and ethnicity have changed over time and across circumstances and how the traditional binary of men and women has been reinforced throughout but also how it has been challenged and altered in various contexts.

data.name: History of the Holocaust
data.description: Explores questions raised by the Holocaust regarding how such barbarism erupted in our so-called civilized and enlightened age. Attempts to analyze the meaning of the Holocaust from three vantage points: that of European, and more particularly, German history; that of Jewish history; and that of those states and religious institutions which shared responsibility. If unable to enroll because of closed registration please contact the professor and a wait list will be created.

data.name: The Modern Jewish Peace Tradition: From Prophecy to Politics
data.description: This course explores the evolving meanings of shalom—peace—in modern Jewish thought and political life. Beginning with a brief overview of classical and rabbinic formulations of peace as divine harmony and moral obligation, we trace how contemporary Jewish thinkers, theologians, and activists have reinterpreted peace across contexts of modernity, nationalism, and conflict. From prophetic ethics and utopian socialism to the paradoxes of Zionism and the Israeli peace camp, students engage with theological, anthropological, and political debates that reveal peace as a deeply contested but enduring ideal. Readings combine theology, political theory, ethnography, and peace studies to illuminate how Jewish traditions of dialogue, justice, and moral restraint continue to shape the pursuit of coexistence in Israel/Palestine and beyond.

data.name: History of the State of Israel: 1948 to the Present
data.description: This course surveys the history of Israel from its Proclamation of Independence in 1948 until today. Israel's history has unfolded under the shadow of its prolonged conflict with the Palestinians and its Arab neighbors. At the same time, an entirely new, vibrant and dynamic society and culture has developed there. This course aims to familiarize the student with the major outlines of Israel's development, and with different narratives and interpretations of that history. The reading materials and class discussions will examine not only the Arab-Israeli conflict, but also its influence on Israeli politics, society and culture.

data.name: Intermediate Korean for Advanced Beginners
data.description: As a continuation of KREA0250 (Elementary Korean for Advanced Beginners), this course is also designed for heritage learners of Korean who have a foundational understanding of the language and wish to rapidly advance their proficiency. Focusing more on expanding vocabulary, refining grammar, and enhancing both oral and written communication, the course addresses the specific needs of heritage learners. Emphasis will be placed on conversational fluency, including smooth use of honorifics and cultural literacy. Students will explore authentic materials, engage in structured dialogues, and participate in discussions that connect language learning to their personal and cultural experiences.This course is ideal for learners who want to strengthen their ties to the Korean language and culture while accelerating their language learning journey.

data.name: Business Korean
data.description: For students who are interested in Korean culture in general and business culture in particular, and in improving their Korean language skills in a business context. The course not only focuses on business and economy-related words and expressions, but also on developing learners' confidence in business writing, conversation and presentations in Korean. Enrollment limited to 15. Prerequisite: KREA 0600 or instructor's permission.

data.name: Translating Korean: Fiction, Poetry & Film
data.description: This class explores the theory and practice of translation in the context of Korean cultural production. Each week we shall grapple with a particular issue in translation studies in dialogue with a Korean-language text. By the end of this course students should be able to locate the tools necessary to carry out translations from Korean to English, to demonstrate an understanding of translation as a craft with its own standards, responsibilities, and complexities, and to have completed a significant translation project themselves. Learners of the Korean language who have completed Korean 600 as well as native speakers of Korean are welcome.

data.name: Research and Methodologies in Latin American and Caribbean Studies
data.description: The course will offer students a collaborative learning environment where research ideas can be developed and discussed with fellow students and a range of LACA faculty. During the course, the instructor will facilitate the development of research ideas and expose students to research methods, tools, and resources specific to the study of Latin America and the Caribbean. Moreover, the instructor will train students to develop research and creative projects and enhance their ability to write and communicate their ideas, produce effective and generative research questions, and/or produce innovative creative projects in a robust and convincing manner. In addition, during the semester, students will benefit from interactive workshops led by the Brown Writing Center as well as visits to special collections at the John Hay Library, and guest speakers from different disciplines.

data.name: Independent Readings in Latin American and Caribbean Studies
data.description:
For upper-division students interested in pursuing topics in Latin American and Caribbean Studies not currently taught in the Brown curriculum. Students must have significant prior coursework, language skills, and sufficient background knowledge to put together a comprehensive reading list and to produce a final paper that meets the research requirement in the LACA concentration. Course restricted to S/NC.

Class requirements include weekly meetings with the instructor, reading responses submitted before each meeting, and a self-assessment at the end of the semester by the student. The independent study will culminate in a research paper of sufficient depth and sophistication to meet the research requirement for the concentration in Latin American and Caribbean Studies.

Registration requires a comprehensive reading list developed by the student in consultation with the faculty member and a written agreement on course requirements. The concentration advisor’s approval is required if the course is to count toward the concentration.

No more than two (2) semesters of LACA 1994/1995 may be used toward concentration requirements in Latin American and Caribbean Studies.

data.name: Protest and Dissidence in Iran
data.description: The anti-authoritarian Woman, Life, Freedom movement arose as a reaction to protests, incarceration and death in Iran in the fall of 2022. In this first-year seminar, you will learn about Iran, a country of 87 million people bordered by Iraq and Afghanistan. We will discuss historical events such as the 1979 Iranian Revolution and how they still affect Iran’s relationship to the West. Through a broad selection of texts, film and art, we will explore topics that include corruption, authoritarianism, multiculturalism, race, social class, religion and gender relations in today’s Iranian culture and society. In English.

data.name: Theory and Practice of Intercultural Competence
data.description: What is intercultural competence? What are its benefits in our globalized society? Through readings, discussions, videos and visuals students will learn the theory behind intercultural competence as well as practical aspects about gaining and honing the skills needed for IC. Students will experience how the implementation of IC enhances study, work and internships carried out both globally and domestically. Limited to 40 students. This course is open to all students. It is also the foundational course required of students participating in Brown’s Certificate of Intercultural Competence.

data.name: Seminar in Language Pedagogy and Second Language Acquisition
data.description: This course prepares graduate students in modern languages to understand theories of language pedagogy and second language acquisition. Students will gain both practical knowledge to incorporate into everyday teaching as well as an understanding of which approaches to teaching might be more effective than others in various classroom contexts and why. Undergraduates may enroll with permission of the instructor.

data.name: Intensive Introduction to Latin
data.description: This course offers a rapid introduction to the Latin language and grammar. As a one-semester introduction to material often covered in two semesters, this course carries two credit hours instead on one. The workload for this course is correspondingly heavy; students may expect an average of ten hours of homework—including memorization, practice, and preparation of vocabulary and grammar—per week. There are no prerequisites for this course.

data.name: Essentials of the Latin Language
data.description: Second course in an intensive two-semester approach to Latin. Special emphasis on developing facility in the rapid reading of Latin literature. No previous knowledge of Latin is required prior to taking this two course sequence.

data.name: Introduction to Latin Literature
data.description: Introduction to Latin literature through intensive reading of major authors in prose and poetry with careful attention to grammar and style. Prerequisite: LATN 0100, 0200 or 0110 (or equivalent).

data.name: Tacitus
data.description: We read Book 11 (47-48 CE) during Claudius’ reign as emperor, focusing on the downfall of Claudius’ wife, Messalina, who plots to replace him with her lover, and his depiction of Claudius’ censorship, especially the admission of Gallic citizens into the Roman Senate (and we compare Claudius’ speech on this subject to the parts of the oration that are preserved on the Tabula Lugdunensis, a bronze tablet found in Lyon, France). We also read most of Book 15 (64-65 CE), with its reportage of Nero’s reign, especially the Great Fire of Rome and the Pisonian conspiracy. We consider Tacitus’ masterly style, historiographic methods, Tendenz, and an overarching question: how did the imperial system ever survive these regimes?

data.name: Fortunatus
data.description: Wide reading in the occasional poetry of the most prolific writer of the early Middle Ages, attending to diction, meter, imagery, allusion, and paying special attention to the (homo- and hetero-) erotic pieces written to the poet's friends.

data.name: Plautus
data.description: Plautus’ Menaechmi, Rudens, and Captivi are three Plautine masterpieces. We read at least two, and do so with as much understanding of archaic Latin and theatric convention as we can muster. Students are expected to be in command of Latin syntax and morphology. Plautine metrics are difficult: reading the Latin text aloud is essential. Aspects of performance are given attention: Plautus, himself an actor, wrote his plays to be performed; we, too, must give thought (if not practice) to the Plautine stage and how that affects our understanding. For comparison, we shall have performance readings of Shakespeare’s Comedy of Errors and possibly Shinza the Barber (a Japanese Kabuki play). If it turns out that students have read any of the plays suggested here and prefer another, please let the instructor know as soon as possible—she will try to accommodate!"

data.name: Latin Prose Composition
data.description: Review of the basic tenets of Latin syntax, composition, and style. English to Latin translation exercises will shore up composition skills, as we study the stylistic traits of seven Roman authors: Cato, Caesar, Cicero, Sallust, Livy, Seneca, and Tacitus. The course will proceed chronologically according to author. Class time will be spent on translation exercises and review, as well as the identification of the stylistic and syntactic characteristics of the seven authors under study.

data.name: Horace’s Odes and their Reception
data.description: We will read selections from Horace’s Odes closely, with attention to the lyric traditions in which they were composed and the lyric traditions which they later helped constitute. In addition to questions of style, meter, voice, and history, we will also examine the commentaries, ancient and modern, which have accompanied readers of the Odes. Although the course is primarily aimed at graduate students in Classics, advanced undergraduates are also welcome to participate, as well as qualified graduate students in other humanities programs.

data.name: Introduction to Linguistics
data.description: The ability to speak and understand a language involves having mastered (quite unconsciously) an intricate and highly structured rule-governed system. Linguists seek to model that rule system. This course introduces students to the areas of phonetics (speech articulation and transcription), phonology (the principles that govern how sounds are put together), morphology (the principles governing word structure), syntax (the rule system governing sentence structure), and semantics (the system that relates sentences to meanings).

data.name: Playing with Words: The Linguistic Principles Behind Word Games and Puzzles
data.description: In this course, we explore the use of language in a domain which is fairly unlike ordinary communication: the use of language in wordplay, alternate languages, games, and puzzles. At one level, understanding aspects of linguistic theory can provide insight into the internal workings of these various forms of wordplay. On another level, wordplay and puzzles often intentionally subvert the grammar and other rules of ordinary language use and therefore can provide a unique lens into typically implicit grammatical knowledge speakers possess and deploy in all domains of language.

data.name: Constructed Languages
data.description: This course introduces students to the field of linguistics and language typology (the study of crosslinguistic variation and universals) through the project of creating their own constructed languages—conlangs. Students will learn about linguistic phenomena from a variety of diverse languages across the world, serving as inspiration for various aspects of their own conlangs. Broad topics include phonetics and phonology (the study of speech sounds and how they combine), morphology (the structure of words), syntax (the structure of sentences), semantics (how linguistics forms express meanings), historical linguistics (how language changes over time), and writing systems. We will also cover the history of the art of constructed languages. The course culminates in a descriptive sketch grammar of the new language, which will be gradually built up with regular assignments throughout the semester based on the topics discussed in class.

data.name: Evolution of Human Language
data.description: Where did the human capacity for language come from and why does language have the properties it does? We’ll consider and evaluate several hypotheses for the biological and cultural evolution of language, drawing on evidence from emergent languages across modalities (spoken and signed), other species, genetics and language disorders, experimental methods, and computational models. This course has no pre-requisites and is appropriate for students new to linguistics and evolutionary theory as well as those with strong backgrounds in these areas.

data.name: Morphology
data.description: What are words? Is there evidence for them having internal structure? What is the relationship between what can intuitively be understood as “forms of the same word”? Is there a distinct word-building module of the grammar, or do words simply arise from the interaction of the syntax and phonology? These are the kinds of big-picture questions that morphologists ask. This course offers an in-depth introduction to the field of linguistic morphology, which investigates the form of “words”—whatever those are. We will investigate a number of morphological phenomena in a diverse sample of languages (e.g. allomorphy, suppletion, syncretism, inflection and derivation, morphomes, paradigmatic structure, lexicalism, bracketing paradoxes, the relationship between morphology and syntax/phonology, *ABA phenomena, etc.), culminating with an introduction to Distributed Morphology, a current and highly-influential theory of morphology and the architecture of the grammar.

data.name: Sociolinguistics
data.description: This course is a comprehensive introduction to theoretical frameworks and methods of analysis in sociolinguistics. We will examine how language variation and change can reflect social structures, construct diverse social identities, and allow us to make sense of our surrounding social landscapes. We will first learn how linguistic variation corresponds to large-scale social categories like gender, age, ethnicity, and class. Then, we will turn our attention to how individual speakers use language to construct identities, portray stances, and achieve interactional goals. Throughout the course, we will consider how ideologies about linguistic variation have been used to invalidate particular ways of speaking and disempower speakers of these varieties, and how language can reflect, reinforce, or contest social inequalities.

data.name: Bilingualism
data.description: This course explores key questions about the linguistic competence of bilinguals in comparison to monolinguals. Although many linguistic models assume a monolingual speaker, most of the world’s population is bi- or multilingual. What does it mean to be bilingual rather than monolingual? How does knowing two languages shape linguistic behavior? What are the consequences of bilingualism for both language use and broader cognitive abilities? How do these realities align—or clash—with social attitudes and public policy? Drawing on data from a wide range of bilingual communities, students will engage with research from psycholinguistics and sociolinguistics to begin addressing these questions. Topics include language comprehension and production, acquisition, the mental representation of dual language knowledge, and the cognitive effects of bilingualism. We will also examine the social context of bilingualism, the advantages and disadvantages of bilingualism, and their implications for linguistic theory.

data.name: Screenwriting I
data.description: Screenwriting I introduces the fundamentals of short-form screenwriting through viewings, readings, exercises and assignments. Areas of particular focus include the short as a fully developed and distinctive work of art; visual storytelling, pacing and flow; effective screenwriting techniques, optimizing every word; articulating your personal voice and vision; creating structures that best suit your content and intentions. In class, we will review your writing, lookbooks and group presentations; view and discuss short films; discuss readings; and do writing exercises. Assigned readings include theory, essays, treatments and screenplays. Films will also be assigned for viewing outside of class. This course is limited to undergraduates. S/NC.

data.name: Digital & Cross-Disciplinary Language Arts 1
data.description: Project-oriented workshop for writers and language artists who want to integrate practices from other disciplines as they devise, compose, and make their work. Those with little or no prior interdisciplinary or digital media experience are welcome. Learning is through making, reading, discussion, group projects, collaboration, and practice-based research presentations: integrating anything from multimedia and multimodal writing, to text and image, text and audio, performance, translingual and audiovisual elements, computation, augmented reality, AI generation, language models (LLMs), or something we haven't even thought of yet. Enrollment limited to 17. S/NC.

data.name: Fiction Writing II
data.description: Topics often include stylistic matters related to tone and point of view, and structural matters like controlling switches in time. See general course description above for course entry procedures for all intermediate workshops. Enrollment limited to 17. Instructor permission required. S/NC.

data.name: Digital & Cross-Disciplinary Language Arts II
data.description: Project-oriented workshop for writers and language artists who want to integrate practices from other disciplines as they devise, compose, and make their work. Welcoming those who have taken the introductory course or have some prior interdisciplinary and/or digital media experience. Learning is through making, reading, discussion, group projects, collaboration, and practice-based research presentations: integrating anything from multimedia and multimodal writing, to text and image, text and audio, performance, translingual and audiovisual elements, computation, augmented reality, AI generation, language models (LLMs), or something we haven't even thought of yet. Enrollment limited to 17. S/NC. ---

data.name: Lilith’s Brood: Weird Women’s Writing; Writing Weird Women
data.description: In this class, we consider how the broad category of “genre fiction” has enabled a range of possibilities and weirdness in fiction written by women. Drawing from generic tropes including magical realism, speculative fiction, folklore, horror, and afrofuturism, in this hybrid seminar/workshop, we will investigate how women writers have explored and exploded generic limitations of the supernatural, spiritual, folkloric, and nonhuman. Using these works as models, we will draw on our own cultural myths, inspirations, and the creative wellspring of the natural world in order to experiment with short and longer form writing.

data.name: Place as Force: Worldbuilding in Realist Fiction
data.description: Setting is, perhaps, the most oft-neglected technical element, but in fact, the most powerful work uses time and place as antagonist, as benevolence, as character. This course will deepen your understanding of setting as force, and we will discuss its use in contemporary works of fiction, as well as your use of setting in your own work. We will, together with a map of the United States, create our own fictional American town: you will turn in one piece set in our town and one piece set in the place and time of your choice.

data.name: Writers on Writing Seminar
data.description: Offers students an introduction to the study of literature (including works from more than one genre) with special attention given to a writer's way of reading. This course will include visits to the course by contemporary writers who will read to the class and talk about their work. Enrollment limited to 19 first year students.

data.name: Advanced Fiction
data.description: The writing of short stories or longer works in progress in regular installments, along with appropriate exercises and reading assignments. See general course description above for course entry procedures for all advanced workshops. Written permission required. S/NC.

data.name: Advanced Fiction
data.description: The writing of short stories or longer works in progress in regular installments, along with appropriate exercises and reading assignments. See general course description above for course entry procedures for all advanced workshops. Written permission required. S/NC.

data.name: Advanced Poetry
data.description: Course work includes a body of exercises, close reading of poetry, workshop conversations and conferences. See general course description above for course entry procedures for all advanced workshops. Instructor permission required. S/NC.

data.name: Advanced Screenwriting
data.description: Screenwriting for feature-length and episodic works. Participants should already have experience writing short screenplays and be prepared to develop a longer piece. See the Literary Arts Department website for course entry procedures for all advanced workshops. Work sample and instructor permission required. S/NC.

data.name: How to Tell the Truth: Documentary Screenwriting (ARTS 1017)
data.description: Interested students must register for ARTS 1017.

data.name: People Falling Together and Apart: Writing Romantic Screenplays (ARTS 1018)
data.description: Interested students must register for ARTS 1018.

data.name: Script to Screen: Scene Work
data.description: This intensive script development and directing workshop is designed for students who have some proficiency in screenwriting, and little to no directing experience. Basic shooting and editing skills are helpful, or will be learned in the first half of the semester. The course aims to serve as a two-way bridge, opening screenwriting students up to the possibility of directing their work, while also investigating how acting and directing experiences, and workshopping material with actors, can inform future screenwriting. Activities include learning acting and directing techniques, scene analysis, writing and revising scenes, casting and working with actors, creating a scene through improvisation, and filming practice versions of scenes. Guests will join us for workshops and final reviews.

data.name: Generative Poetry Workshop
data.description: This intensive generative poetry workshop combines in-class workshops, reading and review assignments, craft talks, in-class writing exercises and course portfolios/packets. The in-class workshops will focus on work brought in for discussion and review and will also include in class writing workshops responding to prompts. The course is designed to encourage the generation of new poetry by students and to develop good reading practices to engender good poetry-writing craft.

data.name: Writers-in-the-Community Training & Residencies
data.description: This course will operate mostly “in the field.” We will spend some weeks discussing pedagogical approaches to teaching creative writing in community settings. We will thereafter train in residence, observing a poetry residency at a local elementary school, with visits to other community settings as well (sites to be determined). We will continue to discuss pedagogy, classroom practices and management, administrator-writer relations, and all other necessary logistical planning throughout the semester. By week 7, students will engage in their own writing residencies in pairs or small teams, working in a community setting of their choosing (K-12 school, shelter, library, etc.).

data.name: Memory’s Imagination
data.description: Taking inspiration from the autobiographical work of newly minted Nobel Laureate, Annie Ernaux, we will examine and produce works of fiction, creative narrative non-fiction and hybrid texts that cleave, in that good old double-sense, to personal experience and/or personal passions. Works by Marcel Proust, Virginia Woolf, James Baldwin, Michael Ondaatje, John Keene, Jessica Au and Svetlana Alexeivich will be on the menu to inform and inspire. Students can expect a substantial weekly reading load and should come to each class prepared to discuss the assigned texts and linked creative assignments.

data.name: Creative Process Workshop
data.description: Creative Writing can be understood as a method of turning ideas, sounds, images, snippets, fragments, dreams, research -- and any and all of life's materials -- into fully realized literary works. The "work" of literary works is hidden when we read books and published authors, and thus our sense of how unique and wondrous work is achieved can be underdeveloped or perfectionistic. In other words, when we rely on only a reader's perspective to guide us as artists, it provides little to no sense of artistic path. This workshop is for writers of all levels to explore several works-in-progress in more depth. The result will be an enhanced relationship to the works (and to process) and to a lifelong and sustainable creativity. Becoming a more curious and helpful reader of others is another tangible and valuable outcome. Open to all.

data.name: Conjuring God
data.description: Is there a place for capital-g God in contemporary fiction? If so, how might the concept of God help us explore our individual and collective search for meaning? And what might narratives about encounters with the divine tell us about the idiosyncrasies of belief (in God, in ideas, in the self)? As we read works by Albert Camus, Graham Greene, Shusaku Endo, Marilynn Robinson and Ursula Le Guin, among others, we’ll discuss why writers still attend to notions of the sacred and divine decades after “the death of God.”

data.name: Reading and Making with Large Language Models
data.description: Large Language Models can be used to generate text with a facility that some of us hail as ‘human-level.’ Informed by theory – chiefly linguistic and language philosophical – this workshop seminar allows its participants to explore what we mean when we say that artificial intelligence reads and writes; and what is implied for our own processes of literary or language-art making when we agree or intend to read and write with (and/or against) such systems. Apart from its reading-informed discussion, the workshop will provide time and critique for its participants’ projects, which will engage and experiment with the seminar-generated ideas and actual machine-learned techniques.

data.name: MICRO-UTOPIAS
data.description: This special topics workshop/seminar explores our capacity as poets, writers, and multi-disciplinary artists to dialogue with the world around us through a socially engaged practice. During this course we will study utopian and dystopian literature, world-making poetry, theoretical frameworks, and a variety of social philosophies and creative practices that, in the words of Carol Becker, create micro-utopias: artistic interventions that “attempt to create physical manifestations of an ideal ‘humanity’ in an inhumane world.” We will workshop and activate our creative ideas in a laboratory-like atmosphere. All art-makers are welcome. Be prepared to move, discuss, risk, play, theorize, and revise.

data.name: Temporal Entanglements: Writing Historical Fiction
data.description: “The past is never dead, it’s not even past.” – William Faulkner How do fiction writers go about creating historical narratives? If we use Saidiya Hartman’s words as our starting point, that we live in a kind of “temporal entanglement,” where the past, present and future, “are not discrete and cut off from one another,” how might we write fiction that straddles this simultaneity? In this class, students will research and write a short work of historical fiction. We’ll discuss research methods and sources, while developing supplementary materials, such as timelines, scrapbooks, and working bibliographies, that will serve to anchor our fictional texts. Students will also workshop their research-based stories with the class. In addition, we’ll read a cross-section of historical fictions and study their various approaches. Students can expect a substantial weekly reading load on top of their individual

data.name: The Poetics of Mysticism
data.description: How does one speak the unspeakable? What rhetorical and rhythmical strategies can the poet use to write the ineffable? How can the practice of writing be used not only to document mystical experience, but to cultivate ceremonial attentiveness? This poetry workshop/seminar explores the intersection of mysticism and poetry. We will investigate how writers across traditions (Islamic mysticism, Christian mysticism, Neoplatonism, pantheism, atheistic mysticism) and eras use language to approach the divine. Through a combination of close reading, seminar-style discussion, and creative workshop, students will engage with core mystical concepts (apophasis, union, ecstasy, divine madness, love, the body, suffering, horror) and draw on these themes and strategies for inspiration into their own creative work.

data.name: Microfiction
data.description: Novels unravel years, decades, or even generations, while short stories culminate in singular, brilliant moments of epiphany. Short shorts, paradoxically, are heavily theme-based and thus tend to cover eternity. In this course, we will discuss the thematic underpinnings of micro fiction, and we will visit with the ways in which short shorts are structured differently from longer stories. We will examine the idea of pacing, as it is rendered in micro fiction, so that we might learn how to use such economy of words in our own micro fiction or traditional-length fiction. Each student will turn in at least three pieces of micro fiction or magical realism, and as final projects, you will revise two of these.

data.name: Writers on Writing
data.description: Offers students an introduction to the study of literature (including works from more than one genre) with special attention given to a writer's way of reading. This course will include visits to the course by contemporary writers, who will read to the class and talk about their work. Enrollment is limited to 30 students.

data.name: Pedagogy Seminar
data.description: The Pedagogy Seminar examines ideas about teaching in a literary arts/creative writing environment. The pros and cons of the “workshop”-style will be discussed alongside alternative models, and general topics of exploration will include: creative process pedagogy, writing-to-learn, multi-genre approaches, uses of readings/research, and general classroom management. Designing an inclusive classroom and syllabus as well as exploring generative and innovative practices will be covered as well. A special emphasis will be on preparing students to feel confident and to explore a range of creative process issues. Personal writing as well as syllabus design will be expected.

data.name: Graduate Independent Study in Professional Development
data.description: This half-credit course is for a Literary Arts graduate student not enrolled in a pedagogy seminar. Through this independent study, the graduate student will work with a faculty advisor to prepare for the post-MFA experience. This may include a focus on aspects of teaching, but may also focus on related professional pursuits, such as working in literary agency, publishing and professional writing.

data.name: Analytic Geometry and Calculus
data.description: A slower-paced introduction to calculus for students who require additional preparation. Presents the same calculus topics as MATH 0090, together with a review of the necessary precalculus topics. Students successfully completing this sequence are prepared for MATH 0100. Prerequisite: MATH 0050 or written permission. May not be taken for credit in addition to MATH 0070 or MATH 0090. S/NC only.

data.name: Math Teaching Fellows Program
data.description: The Math Teaching Fellows Program is a semester-long certificate program that provides UTAs with the skills and knowledge required to be an effective UTA in the Math Department. Admission into the program is by application only; participants will hold a UTA appointment in a Mathematics course approved by the Teaching Fellows Coordinators and must therefore be eligible for student employment at Brown during the term. Participants should have completed at least one of Math 0100, 0180, 0190, 0200, 0350, 0520, 0540.

data.name: Single Variable Calculus, Part I
data.description: An intensive course in calculus of one variable including limits, differentiation, maxima and minima, the chain rule, rational functions, trigonometric functions, and exponential functions. Introduction to integration with applications to area and volumes of revolution. MATH 0090 and MATH 0100 or the equivalent are recommended for all students intending to concentrate in the sciences or mathematics. May not be taken for credit in addition to MATH 0060 or MATH 0070. S/NC only.

data.name: Single Variable Calculus, Part II
data.description: A continuation of the material of MATH 90 including further development of techniques of integration. Other topics covered are infinite series, power series, Taylor's formula, polar coordinates, parametric equations, introduction to differential equations, and numerical methods. MATH 90 and 100 or the equivalent are recommended for all students intending to concentrate in mathematics or the sciences. MATH 100 may not be taken in addition to MATH 170 or MATH 190.

data.name: Multivariable Calculus
data.description: Three-dimensional analytic geometry. Differential and integral calculus of functions of two or three variables: partial derivatives, multiple integrals, Green's Theorem, Stokes' theorem, and the divergence theorem. Prerequisite: MATH 100, MATH 170, or MATH 190, or advanced placement or written permission. [MATH 180 may not be taken in addition to MATH 200 or MATH 350.]

data.name: Multivariable Calculus (Physics/Engineering)
data.description: This course, which covers roughly the same material as MATH 180, is intended for students with a special interest in physics or engineering. The main topics are: geometry of three-dimensional space; partial derivatives; Lagrange multipliers; double, surface, and triple integrals; vector analysis; Stokes' theorem and the divergence theorem, with applications to electrostatics and fluid flow. Prerequisite: MATH 100, MATH 170, or MATH 190, or advanced placement or written permission . [MATH 200 may not be taken in addition to MATH 180 or MATH 350.]

data.name: Introduction to Number Theory
data.description: An overview of one of the most beautiful areas of mathematics. Ideal for any student who wants a taste of mathematics outside of, or in addition to, the calculus sequence. Topics include: prime numbers, congruences, quadratic reciprocity, sums of squares, Diophantine equations, and, as time permits, such topics as cryptography and continued fractions. No prerequisites.

data.name: Linear Algebra
data.description: A first course in linear algebra designed to develop students' problem solving skills, mathematical writing skills, and gain facility with the applications and theory of linear algebra. Topics will include: vector spaces, linear transformations, matrices, systems of linear equations, bases, eigenvalues, and additional topics at instructor's discretion. Applications may include differential equations, difference equations, least squares approximations, and models in economics and in biological and physical sciences. MATH 0520 or MATH 0540 is a prerequisite for all 1000-level courses in Mathematics except MATH 1260 or MATH 1610. Recommended prerequisite: MATH 0100 or equivalent. May not be taken in addition to MATH 0540.
